title: Temporal reversibility of a fluid mixture under concentration gradient
summary: A binary fluid mixture in contact with lateral particle reservoirs is considered. By imposing different particle concentrations in these reservoirs, the system can be maintained under controlled non-equilibrium conditions. Previous stochastic approaches have revealed an unexpected property of the system's state trajectory, namely that it remains time-reversible even when the system is driven out of equilibrium. In the absence of relevant experimental evidence, we employ microscopic molecular dynamics simulations to assess the validity of this surprising result. Remarkably, the simulation results unambiguously confirm the prediction of the stochastic analysis.

AI.tldr: Molecular dynamics simulations confirm that binary fluid mixtures under non-equilibrium conditions maintain time-reversible state trajectories, validating previous stochastic predictions.
AI.motivation: To test the surprising theoretical prediction from stochastic approaches that binary fluid mixtures maintain time-reversible state trajectories even when driven out of equilibrium, since experimental evidence is lacking.
AI.method: Employ microscopic molecular dynamics simulations of a binary fluid mixture in contact with lateral particle reservoirs with different concentrations, maintaining controlled non-equilibrium conditions.
AI.result: Simulation results unambiguously confirm the prediction from stochastic analysis - the system's state trajectory remains time-reversible even under non-equilibrium driving.
AI.conclusion: The surprising property of time-reversible state trajectories in non-equilibrium binary fluid mixtures is validated through molecular dynamics simulations, confirming theoretical predictions despite the lack of experimental evidence.

title: Non-Hermitian Causal Memory Generates Observable Temporal Correlations Invisible to Spectral Analysis
summary: We identify a new class of non-Hermitian causal processes that produce statistically significant temporal correlations invisible to conventional spectral methods. Using a generative model with a strictly causal memory kernel, we demonstrate that time-asymmetric stochastic processes naturally yield sharp transitions at characteristic scales that appear as localized structures in similarity space but leave no trace in power spectra. The model predicts an asymmetric transition profile with orientation-dependent asymmetry parameter $A(θ)=A_0\cos(θ+δ)$ and achieves quantitative agreement ($χ^2/\mathrm{dof}=0.50$, $p=0.86$) with high-precision counting experiments exhibiting $p<10^{-15}$ significance. These results establish a fundamental limitation of spectral analysis for non-Hermitian, non-stationary processes and provide experimentally testable signatures of causal memory in open quantum systems.

AI.tldr: Non-Hermitian causal processes produce temporal correlations invisible to spectral methods, with time-asymmetric stochastic processes showing sharp transitions in similarity space but not in power spectra.
AI.motivation: To identify fundamental limitations of conventional spectral analysis for non-Hermitian, non-stationary processes and discover experimentally testable signatures of causal memory in open quantum systems.
AI.method: Use a generative model with strictly causal memory kernel to demonstrate that time-asymmetric stochastic processes yield sharp transitions at characteristic scales appearing as localized structures in similarity space but leaving no trace in power spectra.
AI.result: The model predicts an asymmetric transition profile with orientation-dependent asymmetry parameter A(θ)=A₀cos(θ+δ) and achieves quantitative agreement (χ²/dof=0.50, p=0.86) with high-precision counting experiments exhibiting p<10⁻¹⁵ significance.
AI.conclusion: These results establish fundamental limitations of spectral analysis for non-Hermitian, non-stationary processes and provide experimentally testable signatures of causal memory in open quantum systems.

title: Longest weakly increasing subsequences of discrete random walks on the integers with heavy tailed distribution of increments
comment: elsarticle style, 21 pages, 13 figures and a few tables
summary:
We investigate the behavior of the length of the longest weakly increasing subsequences (weak LIS) of $n$-step random walks with nonzero integer increments $k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$ given by a zero-mean, symmetric heavy tailed mass distribution proportional to $|k|^{-1-α}$ for several values of the real parameter $α> 0$ together with that of the simple random walk ($k=\pm 1$), to which the $n$-step heavy tailed random walks tends when $α> (1+o(1))\log_{2}{n}$.
  By means of exploratory fits, weighted nonlinear least squares, and ANOVA model comparison, we found that the sample average length $\langle{L_{n}}\rangle$ scales like $\langle{L_{n}}\rangle \sim \sqrt{n}\log{n}$ when the distribution of increments has finite variance ($α> 2$) and $\langle{L_{n}}\rangle \sim n^θ$ with a varying exponent $θ> 0.5$ when the variance is infinite ($α\leq 2$). Distributional diagnostics indicate that the bulk of the $L_{n}$ distribution is very well-approximated by a lognormal model, though systematic deviations are observed in the tails.
  Our results corroborate and expand upon previous results for the LIS of other types of heavy-tailed random walks and raise a conjecture as to whether the distribution of $L_{n}$ is given, or can be effectively described, by a lognormal distribution.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates scaling behavior of longest weakly increasing subsequences in heavy-tailed random walks, finding different scaling regimes based on tail parameter α and suggesting lognormal distribution for Ln.
AI.motivation: To understand the behavior of longest weakly increasing subsequences (weak LIS) in random walks with heavy-tailed increments, particularly how scaling differs between finite and infinite variance cases, and to explore distributional properties beyond previous studies.
AI.method: Analyzed n-step random walks with symmetric heavy-tailed increments (mass distribution ∝ |k|^{-1-α}) using exploratory fits, weighted nonlinear least squares, and ANOVA model comparison; studied both finite variance (α>2) and infinite variance (α≤2) cases, including simple random walk as limiting case.
AI.result: Found ⟨Ln⟩ ∼ √n log n for finite variance (α>2) and ⟨Ln⟩ ∼ n^θ with θ>0.5 for infinite variance (α≤2); distributional diagnostics show bulk of Ln distribution well-approximated by lognormal model with systematic deviations in tails.
AI.conclusion: Results corroborate and expand previous findings on LIS in heavy-tailed random walks; raise conjecture that Ln distribution may be effectively described by lognormal distribution, though further investigation needed for tail behavior.

title: How much of persistent homology is topology? A quantitative decomposition for spin model phase transitions
summary: Point-cloud persistent homology (PH) -- computing alpha or Rips complexes on spin-position point clouds -- has been widely applied to detect phase transitions in classical spin models since Donato et al. (2016), with subsequent studies attributing the detection to the topological content of the persistence diagram. We ask a simple question that has not been posed: what fraction of the PH signal is genuinely topological? We introduce f_topo, a quantitative decomposition that separates the density-driven and topological contributions to any PH statistic by comparing real spin configurations against density-matched shuffled null models. Across the 2D Ising model (system sizes L = 16-128, ten temperatures) and Potts models (q = 3, 5), we find that H_0 statistics -- total persistence, persistence entropy, feature count -- are 94-100% density-driven (f_topo < 0.07). The density-matched shuffled null detects T_c at the identical location and with comparable peak height as real configurations, showing that density alone is sufficient for phase transition detection. However, H_1 statistics are partially topological: the topological fraction grows with system size as delta(TP_{H_1}) ~ L^{0.53} and follows a finite-size scaling collapse delta(T, L) = L^{0.53} g(tL^{1/nu}) with collapse quality CV = 0.27. The longest persistence bar is strongly topological (f_topo > 1) and scales with the correlation length. A scale-resolved analysis reveals that the topological excess shifts from large-scale to small-scale features as L increases. We propose that the TDA-for-phase-transitions community adopt shuffled null models as standard practice, and that H_1 rather than H_0 statistics be used when genuine topological information is sought.

AI.tldr: Point-cloud persistent homology (PH) signals in spin models are mostly density-driven, not topological. H0 statistics are 94-100% density-driven, while H1 statistics contain genuine topological information that scales with system size and correlation length.
AI.motivation: To determine what fraction of persistent homology signals in spin models is genuinely topological versus density-driven, challenging the assumption that PH detects phase transitions primarily through topological content.
AI.method: Introduces f_topo, a quantitative decomposition separating density-driven and topological contributions by comparing real spin configurations against density-matched shuffled null models. Applied to 2D Ising (L=16-128) and Potts (q=3,5) models across ten temperatures.
AI.result: H0 statistics (total persistence, persistence entropy, feature count) are 94-100% density-driven (f_topo < 0.07). Density-matched shuffled null detects Tc at identical location with comparable peak height. H1 statistics are partially topological with topological fraction growing as δ(TP_H1) ~ L^0.53 and following finite-size scaling collapse. Longest persistence bar is strongly topological (f_topo > 1) and scales with correlation length.
AI.conclusion: Most PH signals in spin models are density-driven, not topological. The TDA community should adopt shuffled null models as standard practice and use H1 rather than H0 statistics when seeking genuine topological information about phase transitions.

title: HAMMR-L: Noise Reduction in Quantum Outcomes Using a Richardson-Lucy Deconvolution Algorithm for Quantum State Graphs
summary: Current quantum computers present significant noise, especially as circuit depth and qubit count increase. Prior work has demonstrated that erroneous outcomes exhibit some behavior in Hamming space, enabling improvements in the output distributions of NISQ-era computers. We present HAMMR-L: a principled post-processing technique for improving the fidelity of output distributions by applying Richardson-Lucy image deconvolution on a state graph of measurement results connected by Hamming distance. We show that this preliminary implementation of HAMMR-L outperforms existing cutting-edge Hamming-based post-processors such as QBEEP while being circuit and hardware agnostic, which QBEEP is not. HAMMR-L also demonstrates clear potential for future improvements and we discuss how such improvements might be realized while highlighting the strengths, limitations, and generality of the underlying concept.

AI.tldr: HAMMR-L is a post-processing technique using Richardson-Lucy deconvolution on Hamming-space state graphs to improve quantum computation fidelity in noisy NISQ devices.
AI.motivation: Current quantum computers suffer from significant noise that worsens with circuit depth and qubit count. While prior work shows erroneous outcomes exhibit patterns in Hamming space that can be exploited for output distribution improvement, existing methods like QBEEP have limitations including circuit/hardware dependence.
AI.method: HAMMR-L applies Richardson-Lucy image deconvolution on a state graph where measurement results are connected by Hamming distance. This principled post-processing technique is circuit and hardware agnostic, operating on the Hamming-space representation of output distributions.
AI.result: The preliminary implementation of HAMMR-L outperforms existing cutting-edge Hamming-based post-processors like QBEEP. It demonstrates clear potential for future improvements while being more general and hardware-independent than QBEEP.
AI.conclusion: HAMMR-L represents a promising post-processing approach for NISQ-era quantum computers that leverages Hamming-space error patterns through principled deconvolution. The method's generality, strengths, limitations, and potential improvement pathways are discussed, highlighting its value as a circuit/hardware-agnostic fidelity enhancement technique.

title: The geometric origin of criticality: a universal mechanism in mean-field rotor Hamiltonians
summary:
We introduce a universal criterion for criticality in mean-field rotor Hamiltonians based on the geometric structure of the constant-energy shell. Rather than characterizing the onset of a phase transition through the conventional thermodynamic singularities alone, we show that the relevant information is already encoded in the way the geometry of the shell reorganizes along distinguished collective directions.
  For a broad class of finite-dimensional trigonometric mean-field interactions, the trace of the Weingarten operator (representing the principal curvatures) admits a universal collective expansion in terms of the order-parameter amplitudes. This expansion defines an energy-dependent quadratic form whose eigenmodes identify the geometrically unstable channels of the system. Criticality is then associated with the vanishing of the corresponding curvature coefficients, yielding a direct geometric selection principle for the modes that become unstable at the transition.
  In this way, the phase transition in mean-field systems (usually of first- or second-order) is reformulated as a geometric instability phenomenon intrinsic to the microcanonical energy shell. The resulting framework is geometrically universal within the class considered, independent of model-specific details except for a finite set of collective couplings. Moreover, our approach recovers the known critical channels in standard mean-field rotor models while extending naturally to multimode and spectrally coupled cases. These results support a view in which critical behavior can be understood as reorganizations of energy-shell geometry triggered by a collective restructuring of the underlying energy-shell geometry.

AI.tldr: A geometric criterion for criticality in mean-field rotor Hamiltonians based on the curvature structure of the constant-energy shell, identifying phase transitions as geometric instabilities rather than thermodynamic singularities.
AI.motivation: To develop a universal geometric framework for understanding criticality in mean-field systems that goes beyond conventional thermodynamic singularities, revealing how phase transitions emerge from the reorganization of the energy-shell geometry.
AI.method: Analyze the geometric structure of the constant-energy shell using the Weingarten operator (principal curvatures). Derive a universal collective expansion of its trace in terms of order-parameter amplitudes, defining an energy-dependent quadratic form whose eigenmodes identify geometrically unstable channels.
AI.result: Criticality corresponds to vanishing curvature coefficients of the quadratic form, providing a geometric selection principle for unstable modes at transitions. The framework is geometrically universal within the considered class of trigonometric mean-field interactions, recovering known critical channels while extending to multimode and spectrally coupled cases.
AI.conclusion: Phase transitions in mean-field systems can be reformulated as geometric instability phenomena intrinsic to the microcanonical energy shell, suggesting critical behavior arises from reorganizations of energy-shell geometry triggered by collective restructuring.

title: Quantum Coherence and Giant Enhancement of Positron Channeling Radiation
comment: 4 pages, 3 figures, 1 table. Applies Glauber coherent-state formalism to explain the anomalous peak intensity in SLAC positron channeling experiments (Avakyan et al. 1982); predicts N-to-N^2 intensity scaling as an experimental signature
summary: We present a quantum-mechanical calculation of positron channeling radiation in a planar harmonic potential, explicitly accounting for the interference between transition amplitudes from different transverse energy levels. Because the planar channel potential for positrons in diamond~(110) is well approximated by a parabola, the transverse spectrum is equidistant, $\varepsilon_n = Ω(n+\tfrac{1}{2})$, and all $n \to n{-}j$ transitions radiate at the same Doppler-shifted frequency. The entry of the positron into the crystal under the sudden approximation creates a Glauber coherent state with population amplitudes $c_n$. Phase synchronization between the $c_n$ and the dipole matrix elements ensures that all occupied levels contribute constructively to the radiation amplitude, giving an intensity $I_{\rm coh} \propto |A_j|^2$ that exceeds the incoherent (Zhevago--Kumakhov) result by a factor $\mathcal{G} = 12\text{--}31$ for positron energies of $4\text{--}14$~GeV in diamond~(110). Numerical results agree with the experimental peak positions of Avakyan \emph{et al.}~\cite{Avakyan1982}. The enhancement is unique to positrons because their nearly harmonic channel potential is not replicated for electrons. We propose a decisive experimental test of the coherent model based on the predicted nonlinear angular dependence of the peak intensity. The transition from $N$- to $N^2$-scaling of the radiated intensity, driven by quantum coherence, opens a route toward high-intensity monochromatic gamma-ray sources for nuclear physics and materials science.

AI.tldr: Quantum coherence enhances positron channeling radiation in diamond (110) by factor 12-31 through constructive interference of transitions from Glauber coherent states.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to explain the discrepancy between incoherent (Zhevago-Kumakhov) theory predictions and experimental observations of positron channeling radiation intensity in diamond (110), proposing that quantum coherence effects significantly enhance radiation output.
AI.method: Quantum-mechanical calculation of positron channeling radiation in a planar harmonic potential, accounting for interference between transition amplitudes from different transverse energy levels. The method uses sudden approximation for positron entry creating Glauber coherent states, with phase synchronization between population amplitudes and dipole matrix elements enabling constructive interference.
AI.result: Coherent radiation intensity exceeds incoherent result by factor G = 12-31 for positron energies of 4-14 GeV in diamond (110). Numerical results match experimental peak positions from Avakyan et al. (1982). The enhancement is unique to positrons due to their nearly harmonic channel potential.
AI.conclusion: Quantum coherence transforms radiation intensity scaling from N to N², enabling high-intensity monochromatic gamma-ray sources. The effect is specific to positrons in harmonic potentials, with proposed experimental test based on nonlinear angular dependence of peak intensity.

title: Mean first passage times of velocity jump processes in higher dimensions
summary: First passage phenomena arise across physics, biology, and finance when stochastic processes first reach a threshold, triggering downstream events. Examples include the irreversible exit from a domain, a biochemical reaction, a financial selloff. While typical formulations involve diffusive motion, many stochastic processes are better described as velocity jump processes, characterized by persistent motion interrupted by stochastic velocity changes. Despite their ubiquity, first-passage properties of velocity jump processes remain underdeveloped in higher dimensions, especially under directional bias. We present a general framework to estimate the mean first passage time (MFPT) and higher moments of the survival probability for fixed-speed velocity jump processes where possible reorientations range from strong alignment to full angular anisotropy. For low Knudsen numbers, when the mean free path is small compared to the distance to the target, we derive a universal form for the MFPT in which two bias functions encode broad classes of angular distributions, including von Mises-Fisher, wrapped Cauchy, and elliptical families. In the narrow capture limit of a vanishingly small target, directional persistence induces anomalous scaling, including regimes where the MFPT remains finite whereas standard diffusion would predict divergence. Finally, we obtain a Langevin representation that accurately reproduces first-passage statistics. Analytical predictions are confirmed by numerical simulations.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a general framework for analyzing first-passage properties of velocity jump processes in higher dimensions with directional bias, deriving mean first passage times and higher moments for various angular distributions.
AI.motivation: First passage phenomena are fundamental across physics, biology, and finance, but existing formulations focus on diffusive motion. Many real stochastic processes are better described as velocity jump processes with persistent motion interrupted by stochastic velocity changes. However, first-passage properties of such processes remain underdeveloped in higher dimensions, especially under directional bias.
AI.method: The authors present a general framework to estimate mean first passage time (MFPT) and higher moments of survival probability for fixed-speed velocity jump processes. They consider reorientations ranging from strong alignment to full angular anisotropy. For low Knudsen numbers, they derive a universal form for MFPT using two bias functions that encode broad classes of angular distributions (von Mises-Fisher, wrapped Cauchy, elliptical families). They also obtain a Langevin representation that reproduces first-passage statistics.
AI.result: In the narrow capture limit (vanishingly small target), directional persistence induces anomalous scaling, including regimes where MFPT remains finite while standard diffusion would predict divergence. The analytical predictions are confirmed by numerical simulations. The framework works for various angular distributions and provides universal scaling laws.
AI.conclusion: The developed framework successfully addresses the gap in understanding first-passage properties of velocity jump processes in higher dimensions with directional bias. It reveals anomalous scaling behaviors due to directional persistence and provides accurate predictions confirmed by simulations, offering a comprehensive tool for analyzing such processes across various applications.

title: Geometric structure of the relativistic quantum phase space
summary: The relativistic quantum phase space (QPS) formalism extends classical phase space by incorporating both mean values and variance-covariance matrices of quantum states, thereby providing a unified setting where the uncertainty principle and relativistic covariance coexist. In this work we explore the basic geometric structure of the QPS for the signature \((1,4)\). We construct a scalar invariant built from the mean values and the inverse variance-covariance matrix, and prove its invariance under linear canonical transformations. For quantum states that saturate the uncertainty relations, and define the QPS itself, the invariant takes a value that encodes two fundamental length scales: a large scale characterising maximal coordinate uncertainties and a small scale characterising minimal coordinate uncertainties. From this invariance we derive a geometric equation that unifies the mean values and the quantum fluctuations. Analysing two asymptotic regimes reveals two physically significant limits: one leads to a curved spacetime geometry, consistent with current cosmological observations; the other yields a curved momenta space structure. These limits suggest a direct connection between quantum phase space geometry, cosmology, and quantum gravity, offering new perspectives on the origin of the quantum structure of spacetime. The results also resonate with the principle of Born reciprocity, which posits a fundamental duality between coordinates and momenta, and align with recent works on the relation between QPS and neutrino physics.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a geometric framework for relativistic quantum phase space that unifies quantum uncertainty with relativistic covariance, revealing connections to cosmology and quantum gravity through invariant structures and asymptotic limits.
AI.motivation: To establish a unified geometric framework where quantum uncertainty principles and relativistic covariance can coexist, and to explore connections between quantum phase space geometry, cosmology, and quantum gravity.
AI.method: Construct a scalar invariant from mean values and inverse variance-covariance matrix in relativistic quantum phase space with signature (1,4), prove its invariance under linear canonical transformations, derive geometric equations, and analyze asymptotic regimes.
AI.result: The invariant encodes two fundamental length scales for states saturating uncertainty relations. Asymptotic analysis reveals two physically significant limits: one yields curved spacetime geometry consistent with cosmology, the other yields curved momenta space structure, connecting to Born reciprocity and neutrino physics.
AI.conclusion: The relativistic quantum phase space formalism provides a geometric framework connecting quantum structure, cosmology, and quantum gravity, offering new perspectives on spacetime's quantum origins and resonating with Born reciprocity and neutrino physics.

title: Force Geometry and Irreversibility in Nonequilibrium Dynamics
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures in main text, 1 figure in Supplemental Material
summary: Recent experiments have revealed heterogeneous dissipation in optically trapped systems, often anticorrelated with local positional fluctuations, exposing a structural gap in the scalar stochastic thermodynamic description. While the conventional scalar framework successfully quantifies dissipation through currents and entropy production rates, it does not reveal the underlying vectorial force geometry that shapes spatial dissipation patterns. Here, we bridge this gap by identifying force geometry as an organizing principle for nonequilibrium thermodynamics and introducing force alignment as a geometric determinant of irreversibility. We show that entropy production depends not only on force magnitudes but also on the relative orientation between deterministic driving forces and entropic gradients, vanishing only under exact anti-alignment with matched magnitudes. We formalize this geometric alignment through a time-dependent force-correlation coefficient, quantifying the relative orientation between the forces. This yields an instantaneous geometric lower bound on entropy production that remains valid even when force magnitudes are matched. For overdamped dynamics, perfect anti-alignment defines a thermodynamic stall where net transport vanishes and the lower bound on entropy production is saturated. This force-level perspective provides a structural explanation for the experimentally observed fluctuation-dissipation anticorrelation and nonuniform dissipation. We construct geometric control charts for both constant dragging and sinusoidal driving protocols, explicitly locating experimental operating points within this force-space representation. Together, these results position force geometry as a unifying structural perspective on irreversibility, spanning active biological systems, microrheology, and naturally extending to underdamped dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces force geometry as an organizing principle for nonequilibrium thermodynamics, showing that entropy production depends on both force magnitudes and their relative orientation, with perfect anti-alignment leading to thermodynamic stall conditions.
AI.motivation: Recent experiments reveal heterogeneous dissipation patterns in optically trapped systems that are anticorrelated with local positional fluctuations, exposing a gap in conventional scalar stochastic thermodynamic descriptions that fail to capture the underlying vectorial force geometry shaping spatial dissipation patterns.
AI.method: The authors bridge this gap by identifying force geometry as an organizing principle and introducing force alignment as a geometric determinant of irreversibility. They formalize geometric alignment through a time-dependent force-correlation coefficient that quantifies relative orientation between deterministic driving forces and entropic gradients, yielding an instantaneous geometric lower bound on entropy production.
AI.result: The analysis shows that entropy production depends not only on force magnitudes but also on their relative orientation, vanishing only under exact anti-alignment with matched magnitudes. For overdamped dynamics, perfect anti-alignment defines a thermodynamic stall where net transport vanishes and the lower bound on entropy production is saturated. The force-level perspective explains experimentally observed fluctuation-dissipation anticorrelation and nonuniform dissipation.
AI.conclusion: Force geometry serves as a unifying structural perspective on irreversibility, spanning active biological systems, microrheology, and extending to underdamped dynamics. The framework provides geometric control charts for experimental protocols and positions force alignment as a fundamental determinant of thermodynamic behavior.

title: Securing Elliptic Curve Cryptocurrencies against Quantum Vulnerabilities: Resource Estimates and Mitigations
summary: This whitepaper seeks to elucidate implications that the capabilities of developing quantum architectures have on blockchain vulnerabilities and mitigation strategies. First, we provide new resource estimates for breaking the 256-bit Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem, the core of modern blockchain cryptography. We demonstrate that Shor's algorithm for this problem can execute with either <1200 logical qubits and <90 million Toffoli gates or <1450 logical qubits and <70 million Toffoli gates. In the interest of responsible disclosure, we use a zero-knowledge proof to validate these results without disclosing attack vectors. On superconducting architectures with 1e-3 physical error rates and planar connectivity, those circuits can execute in minutes using fewer than half a million physical qubits. We introduce a critical distinction between fast-clock (such as superconducting and photonic) and slow-clock (such as neutral atom and ion trap) architectures. Our analysis reveals that the first fast-clock CRQCs would enable on-spend attacks on public mempool transactions of some cryptocurrencies. We survey major cryptocurrency vulnerabilities through this lens, identifying systemic risks associated with advanced features in some blockchains such as smart contracts, Proof-of-Stake consensus, and Data Availability Sampling, as well as the enduring concern of abandoned assets. We argue that technical solutions would benefit from accompanying public policy and discuss various frameworks of digital salvage to regulate the recovery or destruction of dormant assets while preventing adversarial seizure. We also discuss implications for other digital assets and tokenization as well as challenges and successful examples of the ongoing transition to Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC). Finally, we urge all vulnerable cryptocurrency communities to join the ongoing migration to PQC without delay.

AI.tldr: Quantum computing threatens blockchain security; Shor's algorithm can break 256-bit ECDLP with <1500 logical qubits; fast-clock quantum computers could enable real-time attacks on mempool transactions; urgent migration to post-quantum cryptography needed.
AI.motivation: To analyze quantum computing threats to blockchain security, provide concrete resource estimates for breaking modern blockchain cryptography, and identify vulnerabilities across cryptocurrency ecosystems that require immediate post-quantum migration.
AI.method: 1) Provide new resource estimates for Shor's algorithm breaking 256-bit Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP); 2) Use zero-knowledge proof for responsible disclosure; 3) Analyze execution on superconducting architectures; 4) Introduce fast-clock vs slow-clock architecture distinction; 5) Survey cryptocurrency vulnerabilities through quantum lens; 6) Discuss technical solutions with public policy frameworks.
AI.result: Shor's algorithm can execute with either <1200 logical qubits and <90M Toffoli gates or <1450 logical qubits and <70M Toffoli gates. On superconducting architectures with 1e-3 error rates, circuits execute in minutes using <500K physical qubits. Fast-clock quantum computers could enable on-spend attacks on public mempool transactions. Identified systemic risks in smart contracts, Proof-of-Stake consensus, Data Availability Sampling, and abandoned assets.
AI.conclusion: Quantum computing poses imminent threat to blockchain security; fast-clock quantum computers could enable real-time attacks; technical solutions must be accompanied by public policy frameworks for digital salvage; urgent migration to post-quantum cryptography is required for all vulnerable cryptocurrency communities.

title: Fundamental problems in Statistical Physics XIV: Lecture on Correlation and response functions in statistical physics
comment: Lecture notes for summer school. Comments are welcome
summary:
In the first part of these short lecture notes, we will present an introduction on (auto-)correlation functions and linear-response functions in the language of a physicist. In particular, the fluctuation-dissipation theorem in classical physics is presented underlining the central role of correlation functions. The fundamental importance of (auto-)correlation functions raises the natural question on how they are characterized in general without referring to the concrete underlying dynamical laws. Perhaps unexpectedly -- despite being elegant and long established in the mathematical literature (Bochner's theorem for correlations; Herglotz-Nevanlinna representations for response) -- this answer is not widely appreciated in physics, partly because the requisite tools lie outside the standard curriculum.
  In the second part we adopt a more rigorous viewpoint: we state the key structural properties of correlation functions and provide selected derivations of these results. Finally, we return to linear response and discuss general characterization results for response functions.

AI.tldr: These lecture notes introduce correlation functions and linear-response functions in physics, discuss the fluctuation-dissipation theorem, and present mathematical characterization theorems (Bochner's theorem for correlations, Herglotz-Nevanlinna for response) that are not widely known in physics.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between physics and mathematics by presenting rigorous mathematical characterization theorems for correlation and response functions that are not widely appreciated in physics education despite being fundamental to understanding these concepts.
AI.method: Two-part approach: 1) Introduction to correlation functions and linear-response functions in physicist's language, including fluctuation-dissipation theorem; 2) Rigorous mathematical treatment stating key structural properties of correlation functions with derivations, and general characterization results for response functions.
AI.result: The paper presents Bochner's theorem for characterizing correlation functions and Herglotz-Nevanlinna representations for response functions, highlighting that these elegant mathematical results provide general characterizations without reference to specific dynamical laws.
AI.conclusion: There exists a gap between physics education and mathematical literature regarding fundamental characterization theorems for correlation and response functions; these notes aim to make these important mathematical tools more accessible to physicists.

title: Color code off-the-hook: avoiding hook errors with a single auxiliary per plaquette
summary: Syndrome extraction in the planar color code is complicated by high weight stabilizers and hook errors that can reduce the circuit-level distance. With a single auxiliary qubit per plaquette, any spatially uniform circuit halves the circuit-level distance. We propose a single-auxiliary syndrome extraction circuit with color-dependent gate schedules that avoids all malign hook errors in the bulk, thereby preserving the full circuit-level distance. The circuit has minimal depth: all stabilizers of the same Pauli type are measured in parallel in six time steps. Furthermore, this schedule can be readily applied to the XYZ color code circuit, yielding an improved temporal distance. We find that at the boundary, no single hook error alone reduces the distance; instead, only certain combinations of hook errors do, which we call fractional hook errors. We demonstrate through Monte Carlo simulations over a range of circuit-level noise models and physical error rates that our circuit outperforms the previous state of the art.

AI.tldr: A syndrome extraction circuit for planar color codes that preserves full circuit-level distance by avoiding malign hook errors through color-dependent gate scheduling, outperforming previous methods in simulations.
AI.motivation: Syndrome extraction in planar color codes is challenging due to high weight stabilizers and hook errors that reduce circuit-level distance, with existing single-auxiliary circuits halving the distance.
AI.method: Proposes a single-auxiliary syndrome extraction circuit with color-dependent gate schedules that avoids all malign hook errors in the bulk, measures all stabilizers of the same Pauli type in parallel in six time steps, and can be applied to XYZ color codes.
AI.result: The circuit preserves full circuit-level distance, has minimal depth, improves temporal distance for XYZ color codes, and outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in Monte Carlo simulations across various circuit-level noise models and physical error rates.
AI.conclusion: The proposed color-dependent gate schedule effectively addresses hook error issues in planar color code syndrome extraction, maintaining circuit-level distance while achieving efficient parallel measurement, representing a significant improvement over existing approaches.

title: Thermalization in high-dimensional systems: the (weak) role of chaos
summary:
In their seminal work, Fermi, Pasta, Ulam and Tsingou explored the connection between statistical mechanics and dynamical properties, such as chaos and ergodicity. Even today, seventy years later, the topic is not fully understood: while most results of statistical mechanics require the ergodic hypothesis to be rigorously proved, there are many indications that these predictions, both in and out of equilibrium, hold even in the absence of a rigorous form of ergodicity.
  Motivated by the above considerations, in this work we reconsider the point of view that the relevant ingredients for the validity of statistical mechanics are the large number of degrees of freedom and the choice of extensive observables, while the details of the dynamics do not play an essential role. This is the idea behind Khinchin's famous proof of the typicality of macroscopic observables at equilibrium. We extend this perspective to the context of non equilibrium, by investigating the thermalization properties of both harmonic (integrable) and nonharmonic (chaotic) oscillator chains initially prepared in out-of-equilibrium conditions. In integrable systems, thermalization occurs, or not, depending on the observable. In the chaotic regime, instead, thermalization is reached by any observable, although the relaxation timescale might be larger than the observation time.

AI.tldr: The paper extends Khinchin's equilibrium statistical mechanics approach to non-equilibrium systems, showing that thermalization depends on observable choice in integrable systems but occurs for all observables in chaotic systems, though relaxation times may vary.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether statistical mechanics predictions hold without rigorous ergodicity, motivated by the Fermi-Pasta-Ulam-Tsingou problem and Khinchin's idea that large degrees of freedom and extensive observables matter more than dynamical details.
AI.method: Extends Khinchin's equilibrium approach to non-equilibrium by studying thermalization in both harmonic (integrable) and nonharmonic (chaotic) oscillator chains prepared in out-of-equilibrium initial conditions, analyzing different observables.
AI.result: In integrable systems, thermalization depends on the observable choice - some observables thermalize while others don't. In chaotic systems, all observables thermalize, though relaxation timescales may exceed observation periods.
AI.conclusion: Statistical mechanics predictions can hold without rigorous ergodicity; thermalization depends on observable properties in integrable systems but is universal in chaotic systems, supporting Khinchin's perspective that large degrees of freedom and extensive observables are key.

title: Quantum Suicide in Many-Worlds Implies P=NP
summary: In this paper we propose a totally serious algorithm to solve NP problems in polynomial time provided one is willing to wager the fate of all observers in the universe on the many-world interpretation of quantum theory being correct.

AI.tldr: A satirical algorithm claiming to solve NP problems in polynomial time by betting on the many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics.
AI.motivation: To humorously explore the intersection of computational complexity theory and quantum interpretations, highlighting the absurdity of certain theoretical claims by proposing a solution that depends on an unverifiable metaphysical interpretation.
AI.method: Proposes a "totally serious" algorithm that leverages the many-worlds interpretation of quantum theory, where computational decisions are made by wagering the fate of all observers in the universe on the correctness of this interpretation to achieve polynomial-time solutions to NP problems.
AI.result: The paper presents a satirical "solution" that theoretically solves NP problems in polynomial time, but only under the condition that one accepts the many-worlds interpretation and is willing to risk catastrophic consequences if it's wrong.
AI.conclusion: This is a humorous thought experiment that critiques over-optimistic claims in theoretical computer science by showing how one could "solve" NP problems by making them contingent on unverifiable metaphysical assumptions with extreme stakes.

title: Enhanced synchronization with proportional coupling in Kuramoto oscillator networks
comment: Main: 5 pages, 6 figures. Supplemental: 8 pages, 3 figures
summary: We introduce a novel coupling scheme for maximizing the synchronization of Kuramoto oscillator networks under a fixed coupling budget. We show that by scaling the interaction strength between oscillators according to their frequency detuning, synchronization is enhanced. The coupling scheme induces a change in criticality, driving the system from a continuous phase transition to an explosive transition by changing a single parameter. Our work offers a general route to efficient synchronization in engineered networks and provides insight into the critical behavior of the Kuramoto model.

AI.tldr: Novel coupling scheme for Kuramoto oscillators enhances synchronization by scaling interaction strength with frequency detuning, enabling explosive transitions with single parameter tuning.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient method for maximizing synchronization in Kuramoto oscillator networks under fixed coupling budget constraints, providing insights into critical behavior and offering practical applications for engineered networks.
AI.method: Introduces a novel coupling scheme that scales interaction strength between oscillators according to their frequency detuning, enabling control of synchronization dynamics with a single parameter.
AI.result: The coupling scheme enhances synchronization and induces a change in criticality, transforming the system from a continuous phase transition to an explosive transition through parameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: Provides a general route to efficient synchronization in engineered networks and offers new insights into the critical behavior of the Kuramoto model through detuning-dependent coupling.

title: Non-stabilizerness and U(1) symmetry in chaotic many-body quantum systems
summary: We present exact, closed-form results for the non-stabilizerness of random pure states subject to a U(1) symmetry constraint. Using stabilizer entropy as our non-stabilizerness monotone, we derive the average and the variance for U(1)-constrained Haar random states. We show that the presence of a conserved charge leads to a substantial suppression of non-stabilizerness (magic) compared to the unconstrained case, and identify a qualitative difference between entanglement and magic response. In the thermodynamic limit, stabilizer entropy exhibits a different leading-order scaling close to a vanishing relative charge density, implying that magic is more robust to charge density fluctuations than entanglement entropy. We test our analytical predictions against midspectrum eigenstates of two chaotic many-body systems with conserved $U(1)$ charge: the complex-fermion Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (cSYK) model and a Heisenberg XXZ chain with next-to-nearest-neighbour couplings and conserved magnetization. We find an excellent agreement for the non-local cSYK model and systematic deviations for the local XXZ chain, highlighting the role of interaction locality.

AI.tldr: Exact closed-form results show U(1) symmetry substantially suppresses non-stabilizerness (magic) in random pure states, with magic being more robust to charge density fluctuations than entanglement entropy.
AI.motivation: To understand how conserved charges (U(1) symmetry) affect non-stabilizerness (magic) in quantum systems, particularly comparing magic suppression to entanglement response under symmetry constraints.
AI.method: Used stabilizer entropy as non-stabilizerness monotone; derived exact average and variance for U(1)-constrained Haar random states; tested predictions on midspectrum eigenstates of chaotic many-body systems: complex-fermion SYK model and Heisenberg XXZ chain with next-to-nearest-neighbor couplings.
AI.result: U(1) symmetry leads to substantial suppression of magic compared to unconstrained case; magic shows different scaling near vanishing charge density, being more robust to charge density fluctuations than entanglement entropy; excellent agreement with cSYK model but systematic deviations with local XXZ chain.
AI.conclusion: Conserved charges significantly suppress non-stabilizerness, with magic responding differently to symmetry constraints than entanglement; interaction locality plays crucial role in determining how well analytical predictions match physical systems.

title: Optimal Control of a Mesoscopic Information Engine
summary: We analytically solve the finite-time control problem of driving an overdamped particle via an optical trap under costly measurement. By formulating this mesoscopic information engine within the Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) framework, we demonstrate that the underlying Linear-Quadratic-Gaussian (LQG) dynamics reduce the optimal measurement and driving protocols to a one-dimensional algebraic Riccati recurrence. From this reduction, we derive the optimal feedback control law for the trap placement, which recovers the discontinuous Schmiedl-Seifert driving protocol in the continuous-time, open-loop limit. We map the operational phase space of the engine, deriving explicit physical bounds on the maximum power that can be extracted from thermal fluctuations. Taking the infinite-horizon limit, we find the exact periodic measurement schedules for the steady-state and derive the macroscopic velocity envelopes beyond which viscous drag forces the engine into a net-dissipative regime. We prove the emergence of deadline-induced blindness, a phenomenon where all measurement ceases as the deadline approaches regardless of their cost. Finally, we generalize the results to a variable-precision sensor.

AI.tldr: Analytical solution for optimal finite-time control of an overdamped particle in an optical trap with costly measurements, deriving optimal feedback control, physical power bounds, and revealing deadline-induced blindness phenomenon.
AI.motivation: To solve the finite-time control problem of driving an overdamped particle via optical trapping under costly measurement constraints, addressing how to optimally extract work from thermal fluctuations in mesoscopic information engines.
AI.method: Formulate the mesoscopic information engine as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with Linear-Quadratic-Gaussian (LQG) dynamics, reducing optimal measurement and driving protocols to a one-dimensional algebraic Riccati recurrence. Derive optimal feedback control law for trap placement and generalize to variable-precision sensors.
AI.result: Derived optimal feedback control law recovering Schmiedl-Seifert protocol in continuous-time limit; mapped operational phase space with explicit physical bounds on maximum extractable power; found exact periodic measurement schedules for steady-state; identified macroscopic velocity envelopes for net-dissipative regime; proved deadline-induced blindness phenomenon; generalized to variable-precision sensors.
AI.conclusion: The analytical framework provides complete solution for optimal finite-time control in mesoscopic information engines, revealing fundamental physical limits on work extraction from thermal fluctuations and novel phenomena like deadline-induced blindness, with implications for optimal measurement strategies in stochastic thermodynamics.

title: Effects of measurements on entanglement dynamics for $1+1$D $\mathbb Z_2$ lattice gauge theory
summary: The $1+1$ dimensional $\mathbb Z_2$ gauge theory is the simplest model that allows for quantum simulation to probe the fundamental aspects of a gauge theory coupled with dynamical fermions. To reliably benchmark such a system, it is crucial to understand the non-unitary quantum dynamics arising from the underlying non-Hermitian evolution and to model the effects of quantum measurements. This work focuses on measuring physical observables for a $\mathbb Z_2$ gauge theory. Tensor network calculations are performed to probe the effect of measurement for larger lattice sizes (up to 256-site systems). Using Matrix Product State calculations, the dynamics of entanglement entropy are studied as a function of the measurement rate and the coupling constant. We find that, under both local and non-local measurements, the late-time saturation value of the bipartite entanglement entropy remains independent of system size, indicating the absence of a measurement-induced phase transition in the no-click limit.

AI.tldr: Tensor network analysis of 1+1D Z₂ gauge theory shows measurement-induced entanglement saturation is system-size independent, indicating no measurement-induced phase transition in the no-click limit.
AI.motivation: To benchmark quantum simulations of gauge theories with dynamical fermions by understanding non-unitary quantum dynamics from non-Hermitian evolution and modeling quantum measurement effects in the simplest Z₂ gauge theory.
AI.method: Tensor network calculations using Matrix Product States to study larger lattice systems (up to 256 sites), analyzing entanglement entropy dynamics as functions of measurement rate and coupling constant under both local and non-local measurements.
AI.result: Late-time saturation value of bipartite entanglement entropy remains independent of system size under both measurement types, indicating absence of measurement-induced phase transition in the no-click limit.
AI.conclusion: The Z₂ gauge theory does not exhibit measurement-induced phase transitions in the studied regime, providing important benchmarking insights for quantum simulation of gauge theories with measurements.

title: Bethe Ansatz with a Large Language Model
summary: We explore the capability of a Large Language Model (LLM) to perform specific computations in mathematical physics: the task is to compute the coordinate Bethe Ansatz solution of selected integrable spin chain models. We select three integrable Hamiltonians for which the solutions were unpublished; two of the Hamiltonians are actually new. We observed that the LLM semi-autonomously solved the task in all cases, with a few mistakes along the way. These were corrected after the human researchers spotted them. The results of the LLM were checked against exact diagonalization (performed by separate programs), and the derivations were also checked by the authors. The Bethe Ansatz solutions are interesting in themselves. Our second model manifestly breaks left-right invariance, but it is PT-symmetric, therefore its solution could be interesting for applications in Generalized Hydrodynamics. And our third model is solved by a special form of the nested Bethe Ansatz, where the model is interacting, but the nesting level has a free fermionic structure lacking $U(1)$-invariance. This structure appears to be unique and it was found by the LLM. We used ChatGPT 5.2 Pro and 5.4 Pro by OpenAI.

AI.tldr: LLM successfully computed Bethe Ansatz solutions for three integrable spin chain models, including two new Hamiltonians, with minimal human correction needed.
AI.motivation: To explore LLM capabilities in performing complex mathematical physics computations, specifically computing coordinate Bethe Ansatz solutions for integrable spin chain models, including unpublished and new Hamiltonians.
AI.method: Used ChatGPT 5.2 Pro and 5.4 Pro to semi-autonomously derive Bethe Ansatz solutions for three integrable Hamiltonians, with human researchers correcting occasional mistakes and verifying results against exact diagonalization.
AI.result: LLM successfully solved all three cases with few mistakes that were corrected by humans. The solutions include: 1) standard Bethe Ansatz, 2) PT-symmetric model breaking left-right invariance, and 3) unique nested Bethe Ansatz with free fermionic structure lacking U(1)-invariance.
AI.conclusion: LLMs demonstrate significant capability in performing advanced mathematical physics computations, successfully deriving Bethe Ansatz solutions for integrable models, including discovering novel mathematical structures like the unique nested Bethe Ansatz with free fermionic characteristics.

title: Quantum Optical Neuron for Image Classification via Multiphoton Interference
summary: The rapid growth of machine learning is increasingly constrained by the energy and bandwidth limits of classical hardware. Optical and quantum technologies offer an alternative route, enabling high-dimensional, parallel information processing directly in the physical layer, particularly suited for imaging tasks. In this context, quantum photonic platforms provide both a natural mechanism for computing inner products and a promising path to energy-efficient inference in photon-limited regimes. Here, we experimentally demonstrate a camera-free quantum-optical images classifier that performs inference directly at the measurement layer using Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference of spatially programmable single photons. Two-photon coincidences directly report the overlap between an input image mode and a learned template, replacing pixel-resolved acquisition with a single global measurement. We realize both a single-perceptron quantum optical neuron and a two-neuron shallow network, achieving high accuracy on benchmark datasets with strong robustness to experimental noise and minimal hardware complexity. With a fixed measurement budget, performance remains insensitive to input resolution, demonstrating intrinsic robustness to the number of pixels, which would be impossible in a classical framework. This approach paves the way toward neuromorphic quantum photonic processors capable of extracting task-relevant information directly from HOM interference, with promising applications in remote object recognition, low-signal sensing, and photon-starved biological microscopy.

AI.tldr: Experimental demonstration of a camera-free quantum optical image classifier using Hong-Ou-Mandel interference to perform inference directly at the measurement layer, achieving high accuracy with robustness to input resolution and noise.
AI.motivation: Classical hardware faces energy and bandwidth limitations for machine learning, while optical and quantum technologies offer high-dimensional parallel processing directly in the physical layer, particularly for imaging tasks. Quantum photonic platforms provide natural mechanisms for computing inner products and energy-efficient inference in photon-limited regimes.
AI.method: Camera-free quantum-optical image classifier using Hong-Ou-Mandel interference of spatially programmable single photons. Two-photon coincidences directly report the overlap between input image mode and learned template, replacing pixel-resolved acquisition with single global measurement. Implementation includes single-perceptron quantum optical neuron and two-neuron shallow network.
AI.result: Achieved high accuracy on benchmark datasets with strong robustness to experimental noise and minimal hardware complexity. Performance remains insensitive to input resolution with fixed measurement budget, demonstrating intrinsic robustness to number of pixels impossible in classical framework.
AI.conclusion: Approach paves way toward neuromorphic quantum photonic processors capable of extracting task-relevant information directly from HOM interference, with promising applications in remote object recognition, low-signal sensing, and photon-starved biological microscopy.

title: Nonequilibrium phases and quantum correlations in synthetic transport models
summary: Quantum devices featuring mid-circuit measurement and reset capabilities, such as quantum computers and dual-species Rydberg quantum simulators, enable the realization of quantum cellular automata. These systems evolve in discrete time following local updates implemented by unitary gates, and allow for the realization of both closed and synthetic open dynamics. Here, we focus on quantum cellular automata that implement minimal models of classical and quantum transport. To illustrate our ideas, we focus on a discrete-time totally asymmetric simple exclusion process and investigate how coherent dynamical contributions allow for the emergence of quantum effects and correlations. We find that bipartite entanglement dominates the transient evolution, while stationary states can retain quantum correlations beyond entanglement. Our results suggest viable routes for realizing transport models on quantum devices and characterizing collective quantum correlations in strongly driven systems.

AI.tldr: Quantum cellular automata implemented on devices with mid-circuit measurement/reset enable study of classical and quantum transport models, revealing transient entanglement dominance and stationary quantum correlations beyond entanglement.
AI.motivation: To leverage quantum devices with mid-circuit measurement and reset capabilities (quantum computers, dual-species Rydberg simulators) for implementing quantum cellular automata that model transport phenomena, exploring how coherent dynamics introduce quantum effects into classical transport models.
AI.method: Focus on quantum cellular automata implementing minimal transport models, specifically using discrete-time totally asymmetric simple exclusion process (TASEP) as illustrative example, investigating how coherent dynamical contributions enable emergence of quantum effects and correlations.
AI.result: Bipartite entanglement dominates transient evolution, while stationary states retain quantum correlations beyond entanglement. The approach provides viable routes for realizing transport models on quantum devices and characterizing collective quantum correlations in strongly driven systems.
AI.conclusion: Quantum cellular automata on measurement-capable quantum devices offer promising platform for studying transport phenomena, revealing distinct roles of entanglement in transient vs. stationary regimes and enabling exploration of quantum correlations beyond entanglement in driven systems.

title: Real Variance-Based Variational Quantum Eigensolver for Non-Hermitian Matrices
summary: Non-Hermitian operators naturally arise in the description of open quantum systems, which exhibit features such as resonances and decay processes, where the associated eigenvalues are complex. Standard quantum algorithms, including the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE), are designed for Hermitian operators and are ineffective in recovering correct eigenvalues for non-Hermitian matrices. We present a systematic formulation based on a Real Variance-based Variational Quantum Eigensolver (RVVQE) for non-Hermitian operators. A correct cost function that guarantees convergence to the true eigenstates is identified. Our implementation utilizes Hermitian measurements only, rendering the algorithm easily deliverable. The performance and scalability of the proposed algorithm on a hierarchy of dense non-Hermitian matrices of increasing dimension are demonstrated with numerical results and computational metrics.

AI.tldr: A variational quantum algorithm (RVVQE) is developed to compute complex eigenvalues of non-Hermitian operators using only Hermitian measurements, overcoming limitations of standard VQE for open quantum systems.
AI.motivation: Standard quantum algorithms like VQE are designed for Hermitian operators and fail for non-Hermitian matrices that naturally arise in open quantum systems, where eigenvalues are complex and describe resonances and decay processes.
AI.method: A Real Variance-based Variational Quantum Eigensolver (RVVQE) is formulated with a correct cost function that guarantees convergence to true eigenstates. The implementation uses only Hermitian measurements, making it practically deliverable on quantum hardware.
AI.result: The algorithm's performance and scalability are demonstrated on a hierarchy of dense non-Hermitian matrices of increasing dimension through numerical results and computational metrics.
AI.conclusion: RVVQE provides a systematic, practical approach for computing eigenvalues of non-Hermitian operators on quantum computers, addressing a fundamental limitation of existing variational quantum algorithms for open quantum systems.

title: Process-tensor approach to full counting statistics of charge transport in quantum many-body circuits
summary: We introduce a numerical tensor-network method to compute the statistics of the charge transferred across an interface partitioning an interacting one-dimensional many-body lattice system with $U(1)$ symmetry. Our approach is based on a matrix-product state representation of the process tensor (also known as influence functional or influence matrix) describing the effect of the bulk system on the degrees of freedom at the interface, allowing us to evaluate a multi-time correlation function that yields the moment-generating function of charge transfer. We develop a scheme to truncate non-Markovian correlations which preserves the proper normalization of the process tensor and ensures the correct physical properties of the generating function. We benchmark our approach by simulating magnetization transport within the Heisenberg spin-$1/2$ XXZ brickwork circuit model at infinite temperature. Our results recover the correct transport exponent describing ballistic, superdiffusive, and diffusive transport in different regimes of the model. We also demonstrate anomalous transport encoded by a self-similar scaling form of the moment-generating function outside of the ballistic regime. In particular, we confirm the breakdown of Kardar-Parisi-Zhang universality in higher-order transport cumulants at the isotropic point. Our work paves the way for process-tensor descriptions of non-Markovian open quantum systems to address current fluctuations in strongly interacting systems far from equilibrium.

AI.tldr: A tensor-network method using matrix product states of the process tensor to compute charge transfer statistics in 1D interacting lattice systems with U(1) symmetry, benchmarked on Heisenberg XXZ circuits showing various transport regimes.
AI.motivation: To develop a numerical method for computing charge transfer statistics in strongly interacting 1D systems far from equilibrium, addressing current fluctuations that are challenging to study with conventional approaches.
AI.method: Matrix product state representation of the process tensor (influence functional) to evaluate multi-time correlation functions yielding the moment-generating function of charge transfer, with a truncation scheme preserving proper normalization and physical properties.
AI.result: Successfully simulated magnetization transport in Heisenberg spin-1/2 XXZ brickwork circuit model at infinite temperature, recovering correct transport exponents (ballistic, superdiffusive, diffusive) and demonstrating anomalous transport with self-similar scaling forms, confirming breakdown of KPZ universality in higher-order cumulants at isotropic point.
AI.conclusion: The process-tensor approach enables study of current fluctuations in strongly interacting non-equilibrium systems and paves the way for non-Markovian open quantum system descriptions.

title: Iterative Optimization with Partial Convergence Guarantees on Neutral Atom Quantum Computers
summary: Neutral atom quantum computers (NAQCs) have emerged as a promising platform for solving the maximum weighted independent set (MWIS) problem. However, analog quantum approaches face two key limitations: constraints of the atomic layout on realizable graph geometries and the absence of performance guarantees. We introduce Lp-Quts, a hybrid quantum-classical framework that integrates an NAQC sampler into a classical cutting-plane algorithm. At each iteration, a relaxed linear program (RLP) bounds the MWIS and induces a reduced graph from which independent sets are sampled using an analog quantum sampler. A novel sample-informed separation problem guides odd-cycle cut selection and accelerates convergence. For t-perfect graphs, Lp-Quts inherits polynomial-time convergence guarantees from the classical theory of cutting planes. We evaluate our approach on instances with up to 300 vertices -- a scale that exceeds the capabilities of current NAQC hardware. In this regime, Lp-Quts reaches solutions within 5--10\% of optimality, outperforming direct analog quantum protocols and greedy baselines under equal sampling budgets. As expected, simulated annealing remains the strongest sample-based solver at this scale. These results demonstrate how quantum samplers can be effectively embedded within classical optimization frameworks to deliver near-optimal solutions with reduced quantum resources while preserving formal guarantees.

AI.tldr: Lp-Quts is a hybrid quantum-classical framework that integrates neutral atom quantum computer sampling into classical cutting-plane algorithms to solve maximum weighted independent set problems with performance guarantees and reduced quantum resources.
AI.motivation: Neutral atom quantum computers show promise for solving MWIS problems but face limitations: constraints on realizable graph geometries due to atomic layout restrictions, and lack of performance guarantees in analog quantum approaches.
AI.method: Hybrid quantum-classical framework integrating NAQC sampler into classical cutting-plane algorithm. At each iteration: solve relaxed linear program (RLP) to bound MWIS, induce reduced graph, sample independent sets using analog quantum sampler, use novel sample-informed separation problem to guide odd-cycle cut selection and accelerate convergence.
AI.result: For t-perfect graphs, inherits polynomial-time convergence guarantees from classical cutting-plane theory. Evaluated on instances up to 300 vertices (exceeding current NAQC hardware capabilities), achieves solutions within 5-10% of optimality, outperforming direct analog quantum protocols and greedy baselines under equal sampling budgets. Simulated annealing remains strongest sample-based solver at this scale.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective embedding of quantum samplers within classical optimization frameworks to deliver near-optimal solutions with reduced quantum resources while preserving formal guarantees.

title: Bell's Inequality, Causal Bounds, and Quantum Bayesian Computation: A Unified Framework
summary: Bell inequalities characterize the boundary of the local-realist correlation polytope -- the set of joint probability distributions achievable by classical hidden-variable models. Quantum mechanics exceeds this boundary through non-commutativity, reaching the Tsirelson bound $2\sqrt{2}$ for CHSH. We show that this polytope structure is not specific to quantum foundations: it appears identically in the causal inference literature, where the instrumental inequality, the Balke--Pearl linear programming bounds, and the Tian--Pearl probabilities of causation all arise as facets of the same marginal compatibility polytope. Fine's theorem -- that CHSH inequalities hold if and only if a joint distribution exists -- is precisely the pivot: the instrumental variable model in causal inference is structurally equivalent to the Bell local hidden-variable model, with the instrument playing the role of the measurement setting and the latent confounder playing the role of the hidden variable $λ$. We develop this correspondence in detail, extending it to algorithmic (Kolmogorov complexity) and entropic formulations of Bell inequalities, the NPA semidefinite programming hierarchy, and the MIP$^*$=RE undecidability result. We further show that the Born-rule / Bayes-rule duality underlying quantum Bayesian computation exploits the same non-commutativity that enables Bell violation, providing polynomial speedups for posterior inference. The framework yields a concrete dictionary between quantum information theory, causal econometrics, and Bayesian computation, and suggests new directions including NPA-based quantum causal inference algorithms and quantum architectures for function approximation.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a formal equivalence between Bell inequalities in quantum foundations and instrumental inequalities in causal inference, showing they arise from the same marginal compatibility polytope structure, with measurement settings corresponding to instruments and hidden variables to latent confounders.
AI.motivation: To bridge quantum information theory and causal inference by demonstrating that the mathematical structures underlying Bell inequalities (characterizing local-realist correlations) are identical to those in instrumental variable models, revealing a deep structural correspondence between these seemingly disparate fields.
AI.method: Develops a detailed correspondence mapping between Bell local hidden-variable models and instrumental variable models, extends this to algorithmic (Kolmogorov complexity) and entropic formulations, connects to the NPA semidefinite programming hierarchy and MIP*=RE undecidability, and explores the Born-rule/Bayes-rule duality for quantum Bayesian computation.
AI.result: Establishes that Fine's theorem (CHSH inequalities hold iff a joint distribution exists) corresponds precisely to the pivot between quantum foundations and causal inference, with the instrumental inequality, Balke-Pearl bounds, and Tian-Pearl probabilities of causation all being facets of the same marginal compatibility polytope as Bell inequalities.
AI.conclusion: Creates a concrete dictionary connecting quantum information theory, causal econometrics, and Bayesian computation, suggesting new research directions including NPA-based quantum causal inference algorithms and quantum architectures for function approximation, while showing that quantum speedups in Bayesian inference exploit the same non-commutativity enabling Bell violations.

title: Can Quantum Field Theory be Recovered from Time-Symmetric Stochastic Mechanics? Part II: Prospects for a Trajectory Interpretation
summary: In a companion paper we derived a unique time-reversal-invariant stochastic generalization of the Liouville equation and showed that it coincides with the evolution equation for the Husimi $Q$-function in a broad class of bosonic quantum field theories. Here we investigate the prospects for interpreting that evolution equation in terms of underlying stochastic trajectories. Drawing on Drummond's time-symmetric stochastic action formalism, we show that the traceless diffusion Fokker-Planck equation defines a natural measure over stochastic trajectories conditional on mixed-time boundary conditions. However, we identify a significant gap: it has not been established that every $Q$-function can be represented as a weighted average of these conditional probabilities over boundary values. The trajectory interpretation holds for ensembles with fixed boundary conditions but does not straightforwardly extend to arbitrary quantum states. Despite this limitation, we show that Drummond's trajectory dynamics are fundamentally non-Markovian -- a natural consequence of combining stochasticity with time-reversal invariance. This non-Markovianity places the dynamics outside the scope of the ontological models framework and thereby explains why the major no-go theorems for hidden-variable theories do not rule out the approach. These results clarify both the achievements and the remaining challenges in the project of understanding quantum field theory as the statistical mechanics of time-symmetric stochastic processes.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether the time-reversal-invariant stochastic Liouville equation for bosonic Q-functions can be interpreted via underlying stochastic trajectories, finding partial success but a key gap in representing arbitrary quantum states.
AI.motivation: To explore the interpretation of quantum field theory as statistical mechanics of time-symmetric stochastic processes by examining whether the previously derived stochastic Liouville equation can be understood through underlying stochastic trajectories.
AI.method: Uses Drummond's time-symmetric stochastic action formalism to show the traceless diffusion Fokker-Planck equation defines a measure over stochastic trajectories with mixed-time boundary conditions, and analyzes the non-Markovian nature of these dynamics.
AI.result: Trajectory interpretation works for ensembles with fixed boundary conditions but cannot represent arbitrary quantum states as weighted averages of conditional probabilities. The dynamics are fundamentally non-Markovian due to time-reversal invariance, placing them outside ontological models framework and explaining why no-go theorems don't apply.
AI.conclusion: While progress has been made in understanding QFT as statistical mechanics of time-symmetric stochastic processes, a significant gap remains in representing arbitrary quantum states via trajectory ensembles, though the non-Markovian nature explains compatibility with quantum mechanics.

title: Oxide-nitride heteroepitaxy for low-loss dielectrics in superconducting quantum circuits
summary: Superconducting qubits show great promise for the realization of fault-tolerant quantum computing, but lossy, amorphous dielectrics limit current technology. Identifying highly crystalline and stoichiometric dielectrics with intrinsically low microwave loss is therefore a central materials challenge, yet experimentally validated platforms remain scarce. In this work, we integrate a crystalline dielectric into a heteroepitaxial TiN/$γ$-Al$_2$O$_3$/TiN trilayer grown via pulsed laser deposition. Correlative high-resolution imaging, diffraction, and spectroscopy measurements confirm the single-crystal quality and chemical integrity of all layers, with minimal defects and limited anion interdiffusion across the oxide-nitride interfaces. Using microwave lumped-element resonators with parallel-plate capacitors, we report the first direct measurement of the dielectric loss of epitaxial $γ$-Al$_2$O$_3$, for which we find a low intrinsic two-level system loss, $δ_{\text{TLS}}^0 = (2.8 \pm 0.1) \times 10^{-5}$. These results establish heteroepitaxial oxides on transition metal nitrides as an attractive materials platform for superconducting quantum circuits, particularly for integration into compact device architectures such as merged-element transmons and microwave kinetic inductance detectors.

AI.tldr: Epitaxial γ-Al₂O₃ dielectric shows low TLS loss (2.8×10⁻⁵) in superconducting qubits, addressing amorphous dielectric limitations.
AI.motivation: Amorphous dielectrics in superconducting qubits cause high microwave loss, limiting fault-tolerant quantum computing. Need crystalline, stoichiometric dielectrics with low intrinsic loss.
AI.method: Grew heteroepitaxial TiN/γ-Al₂O₃/TiN trilayers via pulsed laser deposition. Used correlative high-resolution imaging, diffraction, spectroscopy to confirm single-crystal quality and chemical integrity. Measured dielectric loss using microwave lumped-element resonators with parallel-plate capacitors.
AI.result: Confirmed single-crystal quality with minimal defects and limited anion interdiffusion. Measured low intrinsic two-level system loss: δ_TLS⁰ = (2.8 ± 0.1) × 10⁻⁵ for epitaxial γ-Al₂O₃.
AI.conclusion: Heteroepitaxial oxides on transition metal nitrides are promising materials platform for superconducting quantum circuits, suitable for compact architectures like merged-element transmons and MKIDs.

title: Scalable phonon-laser arrays with self-organized synchronization
comment: 16 pages, 6 figures (SM included). We greatly value feedback, as it provides essential guidance for improving and deepening the quality of our work
summary: Quantum mechanical oscillators operating at frequencies up to the GHz regime have been predicted to support phonon lasing -- self-sustained coherent vibrational motion emerging when the effective gain exceeds intrinsic losses. Current phonon-laser proposals face two key limitations, namely: they lack scalability and rely on coupling all oscillators to a common field, which significantly restricts flexibility and prevents selective, on-demand phonon lasing at specific locations. Given that numerous applications and theoretical insights naturally emerge from scalable many-body systems, addressing these limitations is timely. In this Letter, we demonstrate how scalable arrays of individually addressable phonon lasers can be generated through local driving in a quantum many-body Ising-like spin chain. We rigorously establish the resonance conditions under which mechanical oscillators transition from thermal motion to sustained coherent self-oscillation.
