Unlike previous approaches that rely on a common coupling bus, our proposal employs purely local driving, resulting in an inherently modular and scalable architecture ideally suited for integration into large-scale quantum systems. Additionally, our approach enables on-demand lasing of individual mechanical oscillators at specific sites by simply switching the spin-mechanical coupling interaction on and off, provided specific resonance conditions are satisfied. Notably, our phonon laser array is robust against resonance mismatches and naturally exhibits both pairwise self-organized synchronization and global phase locking near resonance. Finally, we outline an experimental implementation within current experimental capabilities.

AI.tldr: Scalable, individually addressable phonon laser arrays achieved through local driving in quantum Ising spin chains, enabling on-demand coherent mechanical oscillation at specific sites without common coupling bus.
AI.motivation: Current phonon laser proposals lack scalability and flexibility, relying on coupling all oscillators to a common field, which prevents selective, on-demand phonon lasing at specific locations needed for many-body quantum systems.
AI.method: Uses local driving in a quantum many-body Ising-like spin chain to create scalable arrays of individually addressable phonon lasers, with mechanical oscillators transitioning to coherent self-oscillation under specific resonance conditions.
AI.result: Demonstrates scalable phonon laser arrays with on-demand individual oscillator lasing, robust against resonance mismatches, exhibiting self-organized synchronization and global phase locking near resonance, implementable with current experimental capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a modular, scalable architecture for phonon lasers that overcomes limitations of previous proposals, enabling selective control and integration into large-scale quantum systems.

title: High-efficiency and noise-immune quantum battery
summary: Nowadays, quantum batteries (QBs) have been designed to outperform their classical counterparts by leveraging quantum advantages. For instance, the charging power greatly benefits from the entanglement generation of a collective charging scheme (e.g., the Dicke QB), especially in the ultrastrong coupling (USC) regime or even larger. However, apart from the fragility of the QB under intrinsic decoherence effects, another critical drawback emerges inevitably. Specifically, the non-negligible counter-rotating (CR) term in the USC regime would induce coherence in the energy basis of QB, thus remarkably degrading the charging efficiency. To tackle these challenges, we propose a high-efficiency and noise-immune QB boosted by dynamical modulation. It is demonstrated that the time-varying modulation can effectively reduce the CR coupling, resulting in a notable improvement in charging efficiency. Particularly, for a judicious choice of modulation parameters that entirely eliminate the CR interaction, the Dicke QB can be charged optimally, resembling the behavior of the Tavis-Cummings QB. In the subsequent storage process, beyond the natural robustness to pure dephasing noise, our scenario is also highly resilient to the dissipation noise and thus can achieve perfect energy storage by effective bath engineering. While feasible with current experimental platforms, our proposal offers a solid foundation for the implementation of a powerful QB and may drastically promote the development of energy storage and delivery techniques in the future.

AI.tldr: Dynamical modulation boosts quantum battery efficiency by suppressing counter-rotating terms in ultrastrong coupling regime, enabling optimal charging and robust energy storage against noise.
AI.motivation: Quantum batteries in ultrastrong coupling regimes suffer from degraded charging efficiency due to counter-rotating terms that induce coherence in the energy basis, in addition to being vulnerable to decoherence effects.
AI.method: Proposes a dynamically modulated quantum battery scheme that applies time-varying modulation to effectively reduce counter-rotating coupling, enabling optimal charging similar to Tavis-Cummings QB behavior.
AI.result: Dynamical modulation significantly improves charging efficiency by suppressing counter-rotating interactions, and the scheme demonstrates robustness against both pure dephasing and dissipation noise during storage.
AI.conclusion: The dynamically modulated quantum battery offers high-efficiency charging and noise-immune storage, providing a practical foundation for implementing powerful quantum energy storage systems with current experimental platforms.

title: Quantum heat transport in nonequilibrium anisotropic Dicke model
summary: Nonequilibrium heat transport and quantum thermodynamics in light-matter interacting systems have received increasing attention. Quantum thermal devices, e.g., heat valve and head diode, have been realized. Recently, it has been discovered that the anisotropic light-matter interactions can greatly modify the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of hybrid quantum systems, leading to nontrivial quantum phase transitions, quantum metrology, and nonclassicality of photons. To explore the influences of anisotropic light-matter interactions on quantum transport, we investigate heat flow in the nonequilibrium anisotropic Dicke model. In this model, an ensemble of qubits collectively interacts with an anisotropic photon field. Each component interacts with bosonic thermal reservoirs. Quantum dressed master equation (DME) is included to properly study dissipative dynamics of the anisotropic Dicke model. Within the eigenbasis of the reduced anisotropic Dicke system, strong qubit-photon couplings can be properly handled. Our results demonstrate that anisotropic qubit-photon interactions are crucial for modulating steady-state heat flow. In particular, it is found that under strong coupling the heat flow is dramatically suppressed by a large anisotropic qubit-photon factor. While under moderate coupling, the anisotropic qubit-photon interactions enhance the heat flow. Moreover, the increase in the number of qubits amplifies the flow characteristics, with the peaks increasing and the valleys decreasing. Besides, we derive two analytical expressions of heat flows in thermodynamic limit approximation with limiting anisotropic factors. These heat currents exhibit the cotunneling heat transport pictures. They also serve as the upper boundaries for the heat flows in the finite-size anisotropic Dicke model. We also analyze the thermal rectification effect in the anisotropic Dicke model.

AI.tldr: Anisotropic light-matter interactions in the nonequilibrium Dicke model dramatically modulate heat flow, with strong coupling suppressing and moderate coupling enhancing transport, while increasing qubit count amplifies flow characteristics.
AI.motivation: To explore how anisotropic light-matter interactions influence quantum heat transport, building on recent discoveries that such anisotropy modifies hybrid system properties and enables quantum thermal devices like heat valves and diodes.
AI.method: Investigate heat flow in nonequilibrium anisotropic Dicke model using quantum dressed master equation (DME) within eigenbasis of reduced anisotropic Dicke system to properly handle strong qubit-photon couplings.
AI.result: Anisotropic interactions crucially modulate steady-state heat flow: strong coupling dramatically suppresses heat flow with large anisotropic factor, while moderate coupling enhances it. Increasing qubit number amplifies flow characteristics (peaks increase, valleys decrease). Derived analytical heat flow expressions in thermodynamic limit approximation show cotunneling transport and serve as upper boundaries for finite-size systems. Thermal rectification effect also analyzed.
AI.conclusion: Anisotropic qubit-photon interactions are essential for controlling quantum heat transport in light-matter systems, with coupling strength determining whether flow is suppressed or enhanced, and system size amplifying these effects.

title: Calculating the quantum Fisher information via the truncated Wigner method
summary: In this work, we propose new methods of parameter estimation using stochastic sampling quantum phase-space simulations. We show that it is possible to compute the quantum Fisher information (QFI) from semiclassical stochastic samples using the Truncated Wigner Approximation (TWA). This method extends the class of quantum systems whose fundamental sensitivity limit can be computed efficiently to any system that can be modelled using the TWA, allowing the analysis of more meteorologically useful quantum states. We illustrate this approach with examples, including a system that evolves outside the spin-squeezing regime, where the method of moments fails.

AI.tldr: New method computes quantum Fisher information from semiclassical stochastic samples using Truncated Wigner Approximation, extending efficient sensitivity limit analysis to TWA-modelable systems.
AI.motivation: Current methods for computing fundamental sensitivity limits in quantum systems have limitations, particularly for systems that evolve outside the spin-squeezing regime where traditional methods like method of moments fail. There's a need to extend efficient quantum Fisher information computation to broader classes of quantum systems.
AI.method: Proposes using stochastic sampling quantum phase-space simulations with the Truncated Wigner Approximation (TWA) to compute quantum Fisher information from semiclassical stochastic samples. This approach leverages TWA's ability to model quantum systems through stochastic phase-space trajectories.
AI.result: Demonstrates that quantum Fisher information can be computed from semiclassical stochastic samples using TWA. Extends the class of quantum systems whose fundamental sensitivity limits can be computed efficiently to any system that can be modeled using TWA, enabling analysis of more meteorologically useful quantum states.
AI.conclusion: The TWA-based stochastic sampling approach provides an efficient method for computing quantum Fisher information and fundamental sensitivity limits for a broader range of quantum systems, including those outside the spin-squeezing regime where traditional methods fail.

title: Direct measurement of the energy spectrum of a quantum dot qubit
summary: The mapping between gate voltages applied to a double quantum dot, and the parameters of a Hubbard-like Hamiltonian, is of utmost importance for understanding and operating spin qubits. State-of-the-art techniques for measuring Hamiltonian parameters (e.g., detuning axis pulsed spectroscopy, DAPS) provide details about energy levels; however, tunnel coupling estimates typically reveal only a small portion of the full Hamiltonian. Here, we demonstrate a Hamiltonian-agnostic technique for measuring the double dot energy spectrum over a wide energy range, at every value of the detuning, called delta-axis spectroscopy (DAXS). We apply the DAXS method to obtain the energy spectrum of a Si/SiGe double quantum dot and use this data to extract the diagonal and off-diagonal couplings of a 15-level Hubbard-like Hamiltonian, demonstrating very good agreement with the experimental measurements.

AI.tldr: DAXS method enables comprehensive measurement of double quantum dot energy spectrum across all detuning values, allowing extraction of full Hubbard-like Hamiltonian parameters including diagonal and off-diagonal couplings.
AI.motivation: Existing techniques like DAPS provide limited Hamiltonian parameter information, particularly for tunnel couplings, creating a need for more comprehensive measurement methods to understand and operate spin qubits effectively.
AI.method: Delta-axis spectroscopy (DAXS) - a Hamiltonian-agnostic technique that measures the double dot energy spectrum over a wide energy range at every detuning value, applied to Si/SiGe double quantum dots.
AI.result: Successfully extracted diagonal and off-diagonal couplings of a 15-level Hubbard-like Hamiltonian from experimental measurements, demonstrating very good agreement between model and data.
AI.conclusion: DAXS provides a comprehensive approach for measuring the full Hamiltonian parameters of double quantum dots, overcoming limitations of existing techniques and enabling better understanding and operation of spin qubits.

title: From Promises to Totality: A Framework for Ruling Out Quantum Speedups
summary:
We study when partial Boolean functions can (and cannot) exhibit superpolynomial quantum query speedups, and develop a general framework for ruling out such speedups via two complementary lenses: promise-aware complexity measures and function completions.
  First, we introduce promise versions of standard combinatorial measures (including block sensitivity and related variants) and prove that if the relevant promise and completion measures collapse, then deterministic and quantum query complexities are necessarily polynomially related, i.e., $D(f)=poly(Q(f))$. We then analyze structured families of promises, including symmetric partial functions and promises supported on Hamming slices, obtaining sharp (up to polynomial factors) characterizations in terms of a single gap parameter for the symmetric case and refined slice-dependent bounds for $k$-slice domains.
  Next, we formalize completion complexity as the minimum of a measure over total completions of a partial function, and show that completability of a measure captures the possibility of superpolynomial quantum speedups. Finally, we apply this viewpoint to derive broad non-speedup criteria for some classes of functions admitting well-behaved completions, such as functions with low maximum influence on both the standard and $p$-biased hypercubes and functions with efficiently identifiable domains, and then show some hardness results for general completion techniques.

AI.tldr: Framework for analyzing when partial Boolean functions can/cannot have superpolynomial quantum query speedups, using promise-aware complexity measures and function completions to derive non-speedup criteria.
AI.motivation: To understand when partial Boolean functions can exhibit superpolynomial quantum query speedups and develop systematic methods to rule out such speedups, addressing fundamental questions about quantum query complexity advantages.
AI.method: Introduces promise versions of combinatorial measures (block sensitivity variants), analyzes structured promise families (symmetric partial functions, Hamming slice promises), formalizes completion complexity as minimum measure over total completions, and applies this framework to derive non-speedup criteria for specific function classes.
AI.result: Proves that if promise and completion measures collapse, then deterministic and quantum query complexities are polynomially related (D(f)=poly(Q(f))). Obtains sharp characterizations for symmetric partial functions (single gap parameter) and refined slice-dependent bounds for k-slice domains. Shows completability captures possibility of superpolynomial quantum speedups and derives broad non-speedup criteria for functions with low maximum influence and efficiently identifiable domains.
AI.conclusion: Develops comprehensive framework using promise-aware complexity measures and completion techniques to systematically analyze quantum query speedups in partial Boolean functions, providing both positive characterizations and hardness results for general completion methods.

title: Entanglement between an NV Center and Chiral Photons in a Topological SWCNT Plasmonic Microtoroid
summary: We present a theoretical proposal for a hybrid solid-state quantum node based on a single nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center coupled to a topological single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) plasmonic microtoroid. The SWCNT ring supports deeply sub-wavelength whispering-gallery-like plasmonic modes that are naturally described within a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid framework. Owing to the closed-ring topology, the cavity spectrum contains a zero-mode sector that is tunable by an external magnetic flux through an Aharonov-Bohm shift. We show that the strongly confined CNT near field can exhibit chiral spin-momentum locking, enabling the two circularly polarized NV transitions to couple selectively to clockwise and counter-clockwise cavity modes, while the parasitic linearly polarized $π$-transition is strongly suppressed by the pronounced anisotropy of the local Purcell enhancement. Based on a tripod stimulated Raman adiabatic passage (STIRAP) scheme, the system can in principle map the NV spin onto a spin-photon entangled state in a deterministic manner, which is then emitted into a side-coupled tapered optical fiber as a tunable flying qubit. We derive the cavity spectrum, the chiral selection rules, the effective tripod Hamiltonian, and the open-system master equation. Quantitative estimates indicate that, under cryogenic conditions and in the overcoupled regime, high-fidelity spin-photon entanglement and in situ magnetic tuning of the emitted photon frequency are in principle achievable. We also discuss a realistic fabrication route for the CNT resonator and deterministic positioning strategies for a single NV center in the CNT near field.

AI.tldr: Hybrid quantum node using NV center coupled to carbon nanotube plasmonic microtoroid enables chiral spin-photon entanglement with magnetic frequency tuning.
AI.motivation: To create a solid-state quantum node that can deterministically generate spin-photon entanglement with tunable photon frequency, addressing the need for efficient quantum interfaces between stationary qubits (NV centers) and flying qubits (photons).
AI.method: Theoretical proposal combining single NV center with topological SWCNT plasmonic microtoroid. Uses Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid framework for CNT modes, Aharonov-Bohm effect for magnetic flux tuning, chiral spin-momentum locking for selective coupling, and tripod STIRAP scheme for deterministic entanglement generation.
AI.result: Derived cavity spectrum, chiral selection rules, effective tripod Hamiltonian, and open-system master equation. Quantitative estimates show high-fidelity spin-photon entanglement achievable under cryogenic conditions in overcoupled regime, with in situ magnetic tuning of emitted photon frequency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid quantum node enables deterministic generation of spin-photon entanglement with frequency-tunable flying qubits, with realistic fabrication routes and positioning strategies discussed for experimental implementation.

title: Change in bit-flip times of Kerr parametric oscillators caused by their interactions
summary: We experimentally investigate how interactions between Kerr parametric oscillators (KPOs) degrade their bit-flip times, where a bit flip is defined as a transition between the two degenerate ground states of a KPO. Interactions between KPOs cause quantum states of KPOs to leak outside the computational subspace, leading to bit flips. Bit flips degrade fidelity and pose a significant problem for KPO-based quantum information processing. We performed an experiment in which a weak microwave signal is injected into one KPO to emulate photon injection from another KPO, and find that the bit-flip time decreases by an order of magnitude due to induced excitations, depending on the frequency and power of the injected signal. Methods to mitigate the decrease in bit-flip times caused by interactions between KPOs are discussed, including adjusting the pump frequencies, coherent-state amplitudes, and couplings between KPOs. These findings provide valuable insights for scaling up KPO-based quantum computers.

AI.tldr: Interactions between Kerr parametric oscillators degrade bit-flip times by causing quantum state leakage outside computational subspace, reducing fidelity for quantum information processing.
AI.motivation: Bit flips between degenerate ground states of KPOs degrade fidelity and pose significant problems for KPO-based quantum information processing, especially when scaling up systems with interacting KPOs.
AI.method: Experimental investigation using weak microwave signal injection into one KPO to emulate photon injection from another KPO, measuring bit-flip time degradation as function of injected signal frequency and power.
AI.result: Bit-flip time decreases by an order of magnitude due to induced excitations from interactions, with degradation dependent on frequency and power of injected signal.
AI.conclusion: Mitigation strategies include adjusting pump frequencies, coherent-state amplitudes, and couplings between KPOs; findings provide valuable insights for scaling up KPO-based quantum computers.

title: On the Entanglement Entropy Distribution of a Hybrid Quantum Circuit
summary: We investigate the distribution of entanglement entropy in hybrid quantum circuits consisting of random unitary gates and local measurements applied at a finite rate. We demonstrate that higher moments of the entanglement entropy distribution, such as a ratio between the variance and the mean and skewness, capture nontrivial features of the measurement-induced dynamics that are invisible to the mean entropy alone. We demonstrate that these quantities exhibit distinct and robust behaviors across the volume-law and area-law phases, and can serve as effective diagnostics of measurement-induced entanglement transitions. We propose a phenomenological model describing the effect of measurements in the area-law regime, which, when combined with the directed polymer in a random environment description of the volume-law phase, well matches numerical simulations across the entire phase diagram.

AI.tldr: Higher moments of entanglement entropy distribution reveal measurement-induced phase transitions in hybrid quantum circuits, with distinct behaviors in volume-law and area-law phases.
AI.motivation: To understand how measurements affect entanglement dynamics in quantum circuits and develop better diagnostics for measurement-induced entanglement phase transitions beyond just mean entanglement entropy.
AI.method: Study higher moments (variance/mean ratio, skewness) of entanglement entropy distribution in hybrid quantum circuits combining random unitary gates with local measurements at finite rates, using numerical simulations and phenomenological modeling.
AI.result: Higher moments exhibit distinct, robust behaviors across volume-law and area-law phases, capturing features invisible to mean entropy alone. A phenomenological model combining measurement effects in area-law regime with directed polymer description in volume-law phase matches numerical simulations across the phase diagram.
AI.conclusion: Higher moments of entanglement entropy distribution serve as effective diagnostics for measurement-induced entanglement transitions, revealing richer structure than mean entropy alone and providing insights into measurement effects in different entanglement regimes.

title: Multipartite controlled-NOT gates using molecules and Rydberg atoms
summary: We propose high-fidelity controlled-NOT (CNOT) gates in a hybrid system of polar molecules and Rydberg atoms based on the unconventional Rydberg pumping mechanism. By combining the rich internal structure of polar molecules with the strong dipole-dipole interactions of Rydberg atoms, we realize both two-to-one and one-to-two gate configurations. Numerical simulations show that the gate performance is robust against spontaneous emission from Rydberg states. The approach naturally extends to larger systems, as demonstrated by four-qubit implementations achieving three-to-one and one-to-three CNOT gates with fidelities exceeding 99\%. These results highlight hybrid molecule-Rydberg atom architectures as a promising platform for scalable quantum information processing.

AI.tldr: Hybrid polar molecule-Rydberg atom system enables high-fidelity CNOT gates using unconventional Rydberg pumping, achieving 99%+ fidelity for multi-qubit gates with robustness against spontaneous emission.
AI.motivation: To develop scalable quantum information processing by leveraging the complementary strengths of polar molecules (rich internal structure) and Rydberg atoms (strong dipole-dipole interactions) for high-fidelity quantum gates.
AI.method: Unconventional Rydberg pumping mechanism in hybrid polar molecule-Rydberg atom system, enabling both two-to-one and one-to-two CNOT gate configurations. Numerical simulations assess performance and robustness against spontaneous emission.
AI.result: Achieved high-fidelity CNOT gates with performance robust against spontaneous emission. Successfully implemented four-qubit systems with three-to-one and one-to-three CNOT gates exceeding 99% fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid molecule-Rydberg atom architectures represent a promising platform for scalable quantum information processing, with demonstrated high-fidelity multi-qubit gates and natural extensibility to larger systems.

title: YZ-plane measurement-based quantum computation: Universality and Parity Architecture implementation
summary: We define the class of register-logic graphs and prove that any uniformly deterministic measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC) where the inputs coincide with the outputs must be driven on such graphs by measurements in the $YZ$ plane of the Bloch sphere. This observation is revisited in the context that goes beyond uniform determinism, where we present a universal $YZ$-plane-only measurement pattern and establish a connection between $YZ$-plane-only and $XZ$-plane-only patterns. These results conclude the line of research on universal patterns with measurements restricted to one of the principal planes of the Bloch sphere. We further demonstrate, within the framework of the Parity Architecture, that $YZ$-plane patterns with the register-logic graph can be embedded into another graph with purely local interactions, and we extend this case to the scenario of universal quantum computation.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes that uniformly deterministic MBQC with input-output coincidence must use YZ-plane measurements on register-logic graphs, presents universal YZ-only patterns, connects YZ and XZ patterns, and shows how to embed these patterns into locally-interacting graphs for universal quantum computation.
AI.motivation: To complete the research line on universal measurement-based quantum computation patterns restricted to single principal planes of the Bloch sphere, specifically addressing the YZ-plane case and its relationship to XZ-plane patterns.
AI.method: Define register-logic graphs, prove necessity of YZ-plane measurements for uniformly deterministic MBQC with input-output coincidence, present universal YZ-only measurement patterns, establish connections between YZ and XZ patterns, and demonstrate embedding into locally-interacting graphs within the Parity Architecture framework.
AI.result: 1) Uniformly deterministic MBQC with input-output coincidence requires YZ-plane measurements on register-logic graphs. 2) Universal YZ-only measurement patterns exist. 3) Connections established between YZ-only and XZ-only patterns. 4) YZ patterns can be embedded into locally-interacting graphs for universal quantum computation.
AI.conclusion: The research line on universal patterns with measurements restricted to single principal planes of the Bloch sphere is concluded, with YZ-plane patterns shown to be fundamental for certain MBQC scenarios and embeddable into practical architectures with local interactions.

title: PAEMS: Precise and Adaptive Error Model for Superconducting Quantum Processors
summary: Superconducting quantum processor units (QPUs) are incapable of producing massive datasets for quantum error correction (QEC) because of hardware limitations. Thus, QEC decoders heavily depend on synthetic data from qubit error models. Classic depolarizing error models with polynomial complexity present limited accuracy. Coherent density matrix methods suffer from exponential complexity $\propto O(4^n)$ where $n$ represents the number of qubits. This paper introduces PAEMS: a precise and adaptive qubit error model. Its qubit-wise separation framework, incorporating leakage propagation, captures error evolvements crossing spatial and temporal domains. Utilizing repetition-code experiment datasets, PAEMS effectively identifies the intrinsic qubit errors through an end-to-end optimization pipeline. Experiments on IBM's QPUs have demonstrated a 19.5$\times$, 9.3$\times$, and 5.2$\times$ reduction in timelike, spacelike, and spacetime error correlation, respectively, surpassing all of the previous works. It also outperforms the accuracy of Google's SI1000 error model by 58$\sim$73\% on multiple quantum platforms, including IBM's Brisbane, Sherbrooke, and Torino, as well as China Mobile's Wuyue and QuantumCTek's Tianyan.

AI.tldr: PAEMS introduces a precise and adaptive qubit error model that outperforms existing methods by capturing spatial-temporal error correlations through qubit-wise separation with leakage propagation, achieving significant error correlation reductions and superior accuracy across multiple quantum platforms.
AI.motivation: Superconducting QPUs cannot generate massive datasets for QEC due to hardware limitations, forcing decoders to rely on synthetic data from error models. Existing models have limitations: classic depolarizing models lack accuracy, while coherent density matrix methods suffer from exponential complexity (O(4^n)).
AI.method: PAEMS uses a qubit-wise separation framework with leakage propagation to capture error evolvements across spatial and temporal domains. It employs an end-to-end optimization pipeline that identifies intrinsic qubit errors using repetition-code experiment datasets.
AI.result: Experiments on IBM's QPUs show 19.5× reduction in timelike, 9.3× in spacelike, and 5.2× in spacetime error correlation. PAEMS outperforms Google's SI1000 error model by 58-73% across multiple platforms including IBM's Brisbane, Sherbrooke, Torino, China Mobile's Wuyue, and QuantumCTek's Tianyan.
AI.conclusion: PAEMS provides a precise and adaptive error modeling approach that significantly improves accuracy over existing methods while maintaining practical complexity, enabling better quantum error correction through more realistic synthetic data generation.

title: Open Quantum Systems from Dynamical Constraints
summary: Open quantum systems are traditionally described by decomposing the total Hilbert space into a system and an external environment, linked by an explicit interaction Hamiltonian. We propose an alternative framework in which the environment is not introduced as an independent sector a priori, but instead emerges from the dynamical activation of constraints in an initially constrained quantum system. Within Dirac quantization, the physical degrees of freedom define the system, whereas the constraint sector, once promoted to carry its own dynamics, functions as an environment. In this picture, the system-environment coupling is not added through a separate interaction term, but is encoded directly in the constraint structure. As an example, we study a quantum particle coupled to a Brownian-oscillator environment and show how the resulting environmental influence can be formulated in this constraint-based setting. Our results provide a new perspective on the origin of quantum environments and point toward a general framework for open quantum systems rooted in constrained quantization.

AI.tldr: A novel framework for open quantum systems where the environment emerges from dynamical activation of constraints in an initially constrained quantum system, rather than being introduced as an independent sector.
AI.motivation: Traditional open quantum system approaches require explicit decomposition into system and environment with an interaction Hamiltonian. The authors seek an alternative where the environment emerges naturally from the system's own structure through constraint dynamics.
AI.method: Using Dirac quantization, physical degrees of freedom define the system while the constraint sector, when promoted to carry its own dynamics, functions as an environment. System-environment coupling is encoded in the constraint structure rather than added as a separate interaction term.
AI.result: Demonstrated the framework by studying a quantum particle coupled to a Brownian-oscillator environment, showing how environmental influence can be formulated in this constraint-based setting.
AI.conclusion: Provides a new perspective on the origin of quantum environments and points toward a general framework for open quantum systems rooted in constrained quantization, potentially offering more fundamental understanding of system-environment relationships.

title: Junction-Intrinsic Dissipation in Hybrid Superconductor-Semiconductor Gatemon Qubits
summary: Superconducting transmon qubits based on hybrid superconductor-semiconductor Josephson junctions (gatemons) offer gate tunability, but their relaxation times remain well below those of state-of-the-art transmons, and the origin of this discrepancy is not fully understood. Here, we co-fabricate gatemons and SIS-junction transmons with nominally identical circuit layouts, gate dielectrics, and control lines, so that the Josephson element is the only intentional distinction. Across multiple chips, transmons in this architecture reach relaxation times in the tens of microseconds, whereas gatemons saturate in the few-microsecond range. Using the transmons as on-chip references, we construct a loss budget including Purcell decay, spontaneous emission through the control line, and internal dielectric loss, and find that the corresponding T1 limits exceed all measured gatemon values by more than an order of magnitude. Temperature-dependent T1 measurements follow a common quasiparticle-activation model and yield similar superconducting gaps for S-Sm-S and SIS junctions, indicating that the reduced gatemon coherence is dominated by additional temperature-independent, junction-intrinsic dissipation.

AI.tldr: Gatemons (hybrid superconductor-semiconductor transmons) show significantly shorter relaxation times (~few μs) compared to SIS-junction transmons (~tens of μs) in identical circuit layouts, with the discrepancy attributed to junction-intrinsic dissipation rather than conventional loss mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To understand why superconducting transmon qubits based on hybrid superconductor-semiconductor Josephson junctions (gatemons) have significantly shorter relaxation times than state-of-the-art SIS-junction transmons, despite offering gate tunability advantages.
AI.method: Co-fabricated gatemons and SIS-junction transmons with nominally identical circuit layouts, gate dielectrics, and control lines, making the Josephson element the only intentional difference. Used transmons as on-chip references to construct a loss budget including Purcell decay, spontaneous emission, and internal dielectric loss. Conducted temperature-dependent T1 measurements following a quasiparticle-activation model.
AI.result: Transmons achieved relaxation times in the tens of microseconds, while gatemons saturated in the few-microsecond range. The loss budget analysis showed that conventional loss mechanisms (Purcell decay, spontaneous emission, dielectric loss) would allow T1 limits exceeding all measured gatemon values by more than an order of magnitude. Temperature-dependent measurements revealed similar superconducting gaps for both junction types, indicating the reduced gatemon coherence is dominated by additional temperature-independent, junction-intrinsic dissipation.
AI.conclusion: The coherence limitation in gatemons is not due to conventional loss mechanisms or temperature-dependent quasiparticle effects, but rather stems from intrinsic dissipation mechanisms specific to the hybrid superconductor-semiconductor Josephson junctions themselves, which are temperature-independent and junction-specific.

title: Non-perturbative CPMG scaling and qutrit-driven breakdown under compiled superconducting-qubit control: a single-qubit study
comment: 28 pages, 5 figures, 43 pages SI, comments/suggestions are welcomed
summary: Decoherence in superconducting qubits emerges from the interplay of multilevel dynamics and structured environmental noise, yet perturbative models cannot capture all resulting signatures. Here, EmuPlat couples instruction-set-architecture-level waveform generation to the hierarchical equations of motion (HEOM) under $1/f$ non-Markovian pure dephasing. In the resulting non-perturbative regime -- where filter-function predictions become quantitatively uninformative -- CPMG scaling of a three-level superconducting transmon yields one calibration result, two physical findings, and one structural null. Y-CPMG exhibits axis-dependent scaling-law breakdown -- non-monotonic decoherence, partial coherence revival, and pronounced X--Y population asymmetry ($0.204$ vs ${<}\,0.01$) -- driven by third-level anharmonicity amplified by bath memory; X-CPMG maintains well-behaved power-law scaling with a finite-$n$ transient excess consistent with non-Markovian bath-memory effects. The structural null is equally informative: waveform-level differences -- Standard versus VPPU realizations -- remain undetectable across all coupling strengths, establishing that rotating-frame pure-dephasing coupling renders control-layer detail invisible to scaling observables. These findings define testable predictions, the most experimentally accessible requiring only qualitative verification.

AI.tldr: Non-perturbative analysis of superconducting transmon decoherence under 1/f noise reveals axis-dependent CPMG scaling breakdown driven by third-level anharmonicity and bath memory effects.
AI.motivation: Perturbative models fail to capture all decoherence signatures in superconducting qubits, particularly under structured environmental noise and multilevel dynamics. There's a need for non-perturbative approaches to understand scaling behavior in the presence of 1/f non-Markovian pure dephasing.
AI.method: EmuPlat couples instruction-set-architecture-level waveform generation to hierarchical equations of motion (HEOM) under 1/f non-Markovian pure dephasing. This enables non-perturbative analysis of CPMG scaling in a three-level superconducting transmon, comparing Y-CPMG and X-CPMG sequences.
AI.result: Y-CPMG exhibits axis-dependent scaling-law breakdown with non-monotonic decoherence, partial coherence revival, and pronounced X-Y population asymmetry (0.204 vs <0.01) driven by third-level anharmonicity amplified by bath memory. X-CPMG maintains well-behaved power-law scaling with finite-n transient excess consistent with non-Markovian effects. Waveform-level differences (Standard vs VPPU realizations) remain undetectable across all coupling strengths.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals that rotating-frame pure-dephasing coupling renders control-layer detail invisible to scaling observables, establishing testable predictions for superconducting qubit decoherence. The most experimentally accessible predictions require only qualitative verification.

title: Logical-to-Physical Compilation for Reducing Depth in Distributed Quantum Systems
summary: Quantum computing is expected to become a foundational technology for solving problems that exceed the capabilities of classical systems. As quantum algorithms and hardware technologies continue to advance, the need for scalable architectures becomes increasingly clear. Distributed quantum computing offers a promising path forward by interconnecting multiple smaller processors into a larger, more powerful system. However, distributed quantum computing introduces significant circuit depth overhead, as logical operations are typically decomposed into sequential physical procedures that require entanglement generation. These sequential operations limit the reliability of quantum algorithms in the NISQ era due to noise. In this work, we present a compiler that integrates logical-to-physical decomposition with depth-aware rescheduling to reduce the execution cost of distributed quantum circuits. The compiler identifies sequences of logical CNOT gates that share a control or target qubit, reschedules them into parallel instruction groups, and applies decompositions that allow multiple gates to be executed simultaneously using distributed shared entanglement resources. An algorithm is proposed that ensures parallelism is created when possible while keeping logical equivalence and that circuit depth is never increased. Benchmark results demonstrate that the compiler consistently reduces circuit depth for circuits containing inherently sequential CNOT structures, while leaving already-parallel circuits unchanged. These results highlight the value of combining scheduling and hardware-aware decomposition, and establish the compiler as a practical tool for improving the fidelity of distributed quantum computations.

AI.tldr: Compiler integrates logical-to-physical decomposition with depth-aware rescheduling to reduce execution cost of distributed quantum circuits by parallelizing CNOT gates using shared entanglement resources.
AI.motivation: Distributed quantum computing introduces significant circuit depth overhead due to sequential decomposition of logical operations requiring entanglement generation, limiting reliability in NISQ era due to noise.
AI.method: Compiler identifies sequences of logical CNOT gates sharing control/target qubits, reschedules them into parallel instruction groups, and applies decompositions allowing simultaneous execution using distributed shared entanglement resources while ensuring logical equivalence and never increasing circuit depth.
AI.result: Benchmark results show compiler consistently reduces circuit depth for circuits containing inherently sequential CNOT structures while leaving already-parallel circuits unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Combining scheduling and hardware-aware decomposition is valuable for improving fidelity of distributed quantum computations, establishing the compiler as a practical tool.

title: Reducing Complexity for Quantum Approaches in Train Load Optimization
summary: Efficiently planning container loads onto trains is a computationally challenging combinatorial optimization problem, central to logistics and supply chain management. A primary source of this complexity arises from the need to model and reduce rehandle operations-unproductive crane moves required to access blocked containers. Conventional mathematical formulations address this by introducing explicit binary variables and a web of logical constraints for each potential rehandle, resulting in large-scale models that are difficult to solve. This paper presents a fundamental departure from this paradigm. We introduce an innovative and compact mathematical formulation for the Train Load Optimization (TLO) problem where the rehandle cost is calculated implicitly within the objective function. This novel approach helps prevent the need for dedicated rehandle variables and their associated constraints, leading to a dramatic reduction in model size. We provide a formal comparison against a conventional model to analytically demonstrate the significant reduction in the number of variables and constraints. The efficacy of our compact formulation is assessed through a simulated annealing metaheuristic, which finds high-quality loading plans for various problem instances. The results confirm that our model is not only more parsimonious but also practically effective, offering a scalable and powerful tool for modern rail logistics.

AI.tldr: Compact mathematical formulation for Train Load Optimization that implicitly calculates rehandle costs, eliminating dedicated variables and constraints for dramatic model size reduction.
AI.motivation: Traditional container loading optimization models require explicit binary variables and complex constraints for each potential rehandle operation, resulting in large-scale models that are computationally challenging to solve efficiently.
AI.method: Introduces a novel compact mathematical formulation where rehandle cost is calculated implicitly within the objective function, eliminating the need for dedicated rehandle variables and their associated constraints. The approach is validated through a simulated annealing metaheuristic.
AI.result: Formal comparison shows significant reduction in variables and constraints compared to conventional models. The compact formulation proves practically effective in finding high-quality loading plans across various problem instances, offering improved scalability.
AI.conclusion: The compact formulation represents a fundamental paradigm shift in Train Load Optimization, providing a more parsimonious and scalable mathematical model that maintains practical effectiveness for modern rail logistics applications.

title: Adiabatic Ramsey Interferometry for Measuring Weak Nonlinearities with Super-Heisenberg Precision
summary: We propose an adiabatic Ramsey interferometry technique for detecting weak nonlinearities with trapped ions. The method relies on using the quantum Rabi model as a probe, which is sensitive to nonlinear symmetry-breaking perturbations. We show that the couplings which arise either from anharmonic terms of the trapping potential or due to higher order terms in the Coulomb interaction expansion can be efficiently estimated by measuring the spin state probabilities alone. We show that the spin signal is amplified by the mean-phonon excitations, which results in the estimation precision reaching the super-Heisenberg limit. Notably, achieving such high-precision estimation does not require specific entangled state preparation and can be reached even for initial thermal motion state. Furthermore, we show that the super-Heisenberg scaling can be observed even in the presence of weak spin-dephasing.

AI.tldr: Adiabatic Ramsey interferometry using quantum Rabi model detects weak nonlinearities in trapped ions, achieving super-Heisenberg precision without requiring entangled states or specific initial states.
AI.motivation: To develop a sensitive method for detecting weak nonlinearities in trapped ion systems, which arise from anharmonic trapping potentials or higher-order Coulomb interactions, without requiring complex entangled state preparation.
AI.method: Adiabatic Ramsey interferometry technique using the quantum Rabi model as a probe, measuring spin state probabilities to estimate nonlinear symmetry-breaking perturbations. The method leverages mean-phonon excitations for signal amplification.
AI.result: Achieves super-Heisenberg limit estimation precision without requiring specific entangled state preparation, works with initial thermal motion states, and maintains super-Heisenberg scaling even with weak spin-dephasing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adiabatic Ramsey interferometry technique provides a practical and robust method for high-precision detection of weak nonlinearities in trapped ion systems, overcoming limitations of traditional approaches that require complex state preparation.

title: Quantum connectivity of quantum networks
summary: The practical utility of a quantum network depends on its ability to establish entanglement between arbitrary node pairs with quality sufficient to execute entanglement enabled tasks. This capability can be assessed globally, through aggregate performance over all node pairs, as well as locally, at the level of individual nodes. Since entanglement-based connections form a layer above the underlying physical topology, quantum connectivity is not adequately captured by classical topological connectivity metrics. To enable characterisation of the quantum connectivity at the level of the network (or its subnetworks), we introduce the quantum connectivity measure (QCM), which quantifies the average connection quality between pairs of network nodes. Further, we describe two quantities, the quantum-connected fraction (QCF) and the quantum clustering coefficient (QCC), naturally derived from the QCM, which capture important features of the functional connectivity of the quantum network at the level of the network and an individual node, respectively. These metrics of quantum connectivity depend crucially on the entanglement distribution protocol and the quantum network parameters in addition to its physical topology. We demonstrate the crucial distinction between topological and quantum connectivity, showing that even a fully connected graph can be functionally disconnected for quantum tasks if average network edge-concurrence falls below a critical threshold. These quantum connectivity metrics thus provide important tools for the design, optimization, and benchmarking of future quantum networks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces quantum connectivity metrics (QCM, QCF, QCC) to characterize entanglement-based connectivity in quantum networks, distinguishing it from classical topological connectivity.
AI.motivation: Classical topological connectivity metrics are inadequate for quantum networks because entanglement-based connections form a layer above the physical topology. There's a need for metrics that capture the functional connectivity of quantum networks for entanglement-enabled tasks.
AI.method: Introduces the quantum connectivity measure (QCM) to quantify average connection quality between node pairs, and derives two related quantities: quantum-connected fraction (QCF) for network-level connectivity and quantum clustering coefficient (QCC) for individual node-level connectivity.
AI.result: Demonstrates crucial distinction between topological and quantum connectivity, showing that even fully connected graphs can be functionally disconnected for quantum tasks if average network edge-concurrence falls below a critical threshold.
AI.conclusion: The quantum connectivity metrics provide important tools for the design, optimization, and benchmarking of future quantum networks by capturing functional connectivity that depends on entanglement distribution protocols and network parameters beyond physical topology.

title: Entanglement in prepare-and-measure scenarios without receiver inputs
summary: The most elementary prepare-and-measure scenarios have no independent measurement inputs. No inputs mean that quantum advantages require two indispensable ingredients: shared entanglement and measurements that can be adapted to the communicated messages. Understanding these scenarios is therefore conceptually natural, but also practically relevant, since they act as testbeds for black-box certification of adaptive one-way LOCC. Here, we study them systematically and reveal several of their basic features. For classical messages, we first identify the minimal scenario with a quantum advantage and show that it is maximised by high-dimensional entanglement. Then, we identify the next-to-minimal scenario, and show that quantum advantages can be propelled by nonlocality of the Clauser-Horne-Shimony-Holt type, which makes this an appropriate setting for certification experiments. Proceeding further, we replace classical messages with quantum messages, but require the receiver to read the message before measuring the entangled particle. We show that this leads to amplified quantum advantages, that are made possible only thanks to non-projective message read-out. This in dispensable role of non-projective measurements challenges the common wisdom that they play a secondary role in revealing the power of quantum correlations in black-box experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically studies prepare-and-measure scenarios without measurement inputs, revealing quantum advantages require shared entanglement and adaptive measurements, with classical messages showing advantages maximized by high-dimensional entanglement and propelled by CHSH-type nonlocality, while quantum messages with non-projective read-out enable amplified advantages.
AI.motivation: To systematically understand prepare-and-measure scenarios without independent measurement inputs, which are conceptually natural for studying quantum advantages and practically relevant as testbeds for black-box certification of adaptive one-way LOCC protocols.
AI.method: Systematic study of prepare-and-measure scenarios with no measurement inputs, analyzing both classical and quantum message scenarios. For classical messages, identifying minimal and next-to-minimal scenarios. For quantum messages, requiring receiver to read the message before measuring the entangled particle, focusing on the role of non-projective measurements.
AI.result: For classical messages: (1) Identified minimal scenario with quantum advantage maximized by high-dimensional entanglement; (2) Next-to-minimal scenario shows quantum advantages can be propelled by CHSH-type nonlocality, making it suitable for certification experiments. For quantum messages: Amplified quantum advantages are possible only with non-projective message read-out, challenging the common wisdom about their secondary role in black-box experiments.
AI.conclusion: Prepare-and-measure scenarios without inputs reveal fundamental features of quantum advantages: classical messages require high-dimensional entanglement and can leverage CHSH nonlocality, while quantum messages enable amplified advantages through non-projective measurements, establishing their indispensable role in revealing quantum correlations in black-box settings.

title: Non-Equilibrium Sock Dynamics: Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking in the Agitated Wash
summary: It is a universal empirical observation that socks become unpaired in the laundry. We propose a quasiparticle theory of sock dynamics in which individual socks are modelled as bosonic excitations of the agitated laundry condensate. The sock dispersion relation is material-dependent: nondispersive materials retain their shape, while dispersive materials give rise to the well-documented phenomenon of sock shrinkage. In the convex regions of the dispersive spectrum, socks undergo Beliaev decay and spontaneously split into two lower-momentum socks, while in the concave regions the dominant process is Landau-Khalatnikov scattering, which degrades socks into lint and loose threads. In addition, the rotating drum creates sock-antisock pairs from the laundry vacuum via the dynamical Casimir effect. The coexistence of these creation and destruction channels gives rise to a fundamental ambiguity: an unpaired sock at the end of a wash cycle is equally consistent with the destruction of its partner or the spontaneous creation of an entirely new sock.

AI.tldr: A quasiparticle theory models socks as bosonic excitations in laundry, explaining sock loss through quantum-inspired processes like Beliaev decay, Landau-Khalatnikov scattering, and dynamical Casimir effect.
AI.motivation: To provide a theoretical framework explaining the universal empirical observation of socks becoming unpaired during laundry cycles, moving beyond anecdotal explanations to a formal physical model.
AI.method: Develops a quasiparticle theory where individual socks are treated as bosonic excitations of an agitated laundry condensate, with material-dependent dispersion relations and quantum-inspired processes including Beliaev decay, Landau-Khalatnikov scattering, and dynamical Casimir effect in rotating drums.
AI.result: The theory explains sock shrinkage through dispersive materials, sock splitting via Beliaev decay in convex dispersion regions, degradation to lint via Landau-Khalatnikov scattering in concave regions, and sock-antisock pair creation through dynamical Casimir effect in rotating drums.
AI.conclusion: The coexistence of creation and destruction channels creates fundamental ambiguity: an unpaired sock at wash cycle end is equally consistent with partner destruction or spontaneous creation of a new sock, providing a formal theoretical basis for the sock pairing mystery.

title: The Manipulate-and-Observe Attack on Quantum Key Distribution
summary: Quantum key distribution is often regarded as an unconditionally secure method to exchange a secret key by harnessing fundamental aspects of quantum mechanics. Despite the robustness of key exchange, classical post-processing reveals vulnerabilities that an eavesdropper could target. In particular, many reconciliation protocols correct errors by comparing the parities of subsets between both parties. These communications occur over insecure channels, leaking information that an eavesdropper could exploit. Currently there is no holistic threat model that addresses how parity-leakage during reconciliation might be actively manipulated. In this paper we introduce a new form of attack, namely the Manipulate-and-Observe attack in which the adversary (1) partially intercepts a fraction $ρ$ of the qubits during key exchange, injecting the maximally tolerated amount of errors up to the 11 percent error threshold whilst remaining undetected and (2) probes the maximum amount of parity-leakage during reconciliation, and exploits it using a vectorised, parallel brute force filter to shrink the search space from 2n down to as few as a single candidate, for an n-bit reconciled key. We perform simulations of the attack, deploying it on the most widely used protocol, BB84, andthe benchmark reconciliation protocol, Cascade. Our simulation results demonstrate that the attack can significantly reduce the security below the theoretical bound and, in the worst case, fully recover the reconciled key material. The principles of the attack could threaten other parity-based reconciliation schemes, like Low Density Parity Check, which underscores the need for urgent consideration of the combined security of key exchange and post-processing.

AI.tldr: A new "Manipulate-and-Observe" attack exploits parity leakage in QKD reconciliation to significantly reduce security, potentially fully recovering reconciled keys.
AI.motivation: Quantum key distribution's classical post-processing reveals vulnerabilities where parity leakage during reconciliation can be exploited, lacking holistic threat models for active manipulation of this leakage.
AI.method: Introduces a two-phase attack: (1) partial qubit interception with error injection up to 11% threshold, and (2) exploiting parity leakage using vectorized parallel brute force filtering to shrink search space from 2^n to potentially single candidates.
AI.result: Simulations on BB84 and Cascade show attack significantly reduces security below theoretical bounds, with worst-case full recovery of reconciled key material.
AI.conclusion: The attack threatens parity-based reconciliation schemes broadly, highlighting urgent need for combined security consideration of key exchange and post-processing.

title: Probes of chaos over the Clifford group and approach to Haar values
summary: Chaotic behavior of quantum systems can be characterized by the adherence of the expectation values of given probes to moments of the Haar distribution. In this work, we analyze the behavior of several probes of chaos using a technique known as Isospectral Twirling [1]. This consists in fixing the spectrum of the Hamiltonian and picking its eigenvectors at random. Here, we study the transition from stabilizer bases to random bases according to the Haar measure by T-doped random quantum circuits. We then compute the average value of the probes over ensembles of random spectra from Random Matrix Theory, the Gaussian Diagonal Ensemble and the Gaussian Unitary Ensemble, associated with non-chaotic and chaotic behavior respectively. We also study the behavior of such probes over the Toric Code Hamiltonian.

AI.tldr: Analysis of quantum chaos probes using Isospectral Twirling technique, studying transitions from stabilizer to random bases via T-doped circuits, and evaluating probes over various random matrix ensembles and the Toric Code Hamiltonian.
AI.motivation: To characterize chaotic behavior in quantum systems by examining how expectation values of probes adhere to moments of the Haar distribution, using Isospectral Twirling to study the transition from structured (stabilizer) bases to fully random bases.
AI.method: Uses Isospectral Twirling technique which fixes Hamiltonian spectrum while randomizing eigenvectors. Studies transition via T-doped random quantum circuits. Computes average probe values over ensembles: Random Matrix Theory (chaotic), Gaussian Diagonal Ensemble (non-chaotic), Gaussian Unitary Ensemble (chaotic), and analyzes the Toric Code Hamiltonian.
AI.result: The paper analyzes how different probes of chaos behave across the spectrum of bases from stabilizer to Haar-random, and evaluates their performance over various random matrix ensembles representing different chaotic regimes, plus the specific case of the Toric Code Hamiltonian.
AI.conclusion: The Isospectral Twirling technique combined with T-doped circuits provides a systematic framework to study quantum chaos transitions, with probes showing distinct behaviors across different ensembles and the structured Toric Code system.

title: Optimal Control of Spin Squeezing in 2D Finite-Range Interacting Systems
summary: Spin squeezing serves as both a fundamental witness of quantum entanglement and a critical resource for quantum-enhanced metrology. While generating substantial spin squeezing in finite-range interacting systems remains challenging, such capability is important for advancing quantum technologies. In this work, we develop an optimal control strategy for achieving enhanced spin squeezing in a two-dimensional XX model with dipolar interactions. Leveraging rotor-spin-wave theory for periodic boundary conditions, we circumvent computational bottlenecks to explore control strategies at unprecedented scales. Remarkably, optimizing a single collective transverse field is sufficient to achieve substantial squeezing enhancement, exceeding the two-axis-twisting benchmark. The optimized control field achieves this breakthrough by dynamically suppressing inter-subspace mixing induced by the finite-range interactions, thereby confining the system evolution predominantly within the maximal spin subspace. We further extend rotor-spin-wave theory to open boundary conditions and incorporate dephasing noise, providing a scalable framework for realistic systems. Under these conditions, the optimized protocol remains effective, highlighting its robustness and suitability for experimental implementation.

AI.tldr: Optimal control strategy achieves enhanced spin squeezing in 2D dipolar XX model by suppressing inter-subspace mixing with optimized transverse field.
AI.motivation: Spin squeezing is crucial for quantum entanglement detection and quantum-enhanced metrology, but generating substantial squeezing in finite-range interacting systems remains challenging, limiting quantum technology advancement.
AI.method: Develop optimal control strategy using rotor-spin-wave theory for periodic boundary conditions to circumvent computational bottlenecks. Optimize single collective transverse field to suppress inter-subspace mixing induced by finite-range dipolar interactions in 2D XX model.
AI.result: Optimized control field achieves substantial squeezing enhancement exceeding two-axis-twisting benchmark by dynamically confining system evolution within maximal spin subspace. The protocol remains effective when extended to open boundary conditions with dephasing noise.
AI.conclusion: The optimal control strategy provides robust, scalable framework for realistic experimental implementation, enabling enhanced spin squeezing in finite-range interacting systems for quantum technology applications.

title: Nonequilibrium energy transport in driven-dissipative quantum systems
summary: Nonequilibrium energy transport serves as one of fundamental problems in quantum thermodynamics and quantum technologies. Driven quantum master equation in the dressed picture provides an efficient way of investigating nonequilibrium energy flow in general driven-dissipative quantum systems, where the systems are simultaneously driven by the finite thermodynamic bias and coherent driving field. The validity and general applicability of driven quantum master equation is confirmed by comparing with Floquet master equation, by analyzing energy currents in generic spin and boson models. The additional driving phase reserved in system-reservoir interactions, will apparently modify microscopic energy exchange processes. The steady-state energy currents are dramatically enhanced, in particular near the resonant regimes. In contrast, the traditional dressed master equation yields distinct behaviors of the energy currents. We hope that the driven quantum master equation may provide an efficient utility for the control of quantum transport and thermodynamic performances in driven-dissipative nanodevices.

AI.tldr: A driven quantum master equation in the dressed picture provides an efficient method to study nonequilibrium energy transport in driven-dissipative quantum systems, showing enhanced energy currents near resonant regimes compared to traditional approaches.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient framework for investigating nonequilibrium energy transport in driven-dissipative quantum systems, which is fundamental for quantum thermodynamics and quantum technologies, particularly for controlling quantum transport in nanodevices.
AI.method: Proposes a driven quantum master equation in the dressed picture that accounts for simultaneous thermodynamic bias and coherent driving fields. Validates the approach by comparing with Floquet master equation and analyzing energy currents in generic spin and boson models, while considering the effect of driving phase in system-reservoir interactions.
AI.result: The driven quantum master equation is valid and generally applicable. The additional driving phase in system-reservoir interactions modifies microscopic energy exchange processes, dramatically enhancing steady-state energy currents, particularly near resonant regimes. Traditional dressed master equations yield distinct energy current behaviors.
AI.conclusion: The driven quantum master equation provides an efficient utility for controlling quantum transport and thermodynamic performances in driven-dissipative nanodevices, offering enhanced energy current manipulation capabilities compared to traditional approaches.

title: Topological sum rule for geometric phases of quantum gates
summary: We establish a topological sum rule, $ν_U = \frac{1}{2π}\sum_nγ_n = 2mν_H$, connecting the geometric phases accumulated by a two-qubit system over a complete basis of initial states to the winding number $ν_H$ classifying its Hamiltonian. Implementations of the same gate from different topological classes must distribute these phases differently, making their distinction measurable through the Wootters concurrence. As a corollary, nontrivial topology is a necessary condition for entanglement: only Hamiltonians with access to $ν_H \neq 0$ can generate it.

AI.tldr: Topological sum rule connects geometric phases of two-qubit systems to Hamiltonian winding number, enabling topological distinction of quantum gates and establishing topology as necessary for entanglement generation.
AI.motivation: To establish fundamental connections between topological properties of quantum systems and their geometric phases, enabling topological classification of quantum gates and understanding the relationship between topology and entanglement generation in quantum systems.
AI.method: Developed a topological sum rule relating geometric phases accumulated by a two-qubit system over a complete basis of initial states to the winding number classifying its Hamiltonian. Used Wootters concurrence to measure phase distribution differences between gates from different topological classes.
AI.result: Established that implementations of the same gate from different topological classes distribute geometric phases differently, making their distinction measurable. Proved that nontrivial topology (ν_H ≠ 0) is a necessary condition for entanglement generation in two-qubit systems.
AI.conclusion: Topological properties fundamentally constrain quantum operations: geometric phases encode topological information, gates from different topological classes are physically distinguishable, and nontrivial topology is essential for entanglement generation in quantum systems.

title: Certifying and learning local quantum Hamiltonians
comment: 27 pages. This work subsumes prior works (arXiv:2509.10239, arXiv:2512.09778)
summary:
In this work, we study the problems of certifying and learning quantum $k$-local Hamiltonians, for a constant $k$. Our main contributions are as follows:
  - Certification of Hamiltonians. We show that certifying a local Hamiltonian in normalized Frobenius norm via access to its time-evolution operator can be achieved with only $O(1/\varepsilon)$ evolution time. This is optimal, as it matches the Heisenberg-scaling lower bound of $Ω(1/\varepsilon)$. To our knowledge, this is the first optimal algorithm for testing a Hamiltonian property. A key ingredient in our analysis is the Bonami Hypercontractivity Lemma from Fourier analysis.
  - Learning Gibbs states. We design an algorithm for learning Gibbs states of local Hamiltonians in trace norm that is sample-efficient in all relevant parameters. In contrast, previous approaches learned the underlying Hamiltonian (which implies learning the Gibbs state), and thus inevitably suffered from exponential sample complexity scaling in the inverse temperature.
  - Certification of Gibbs states. We give an algorithm for certifying Gibbs states of local Hamiltonians in trace norm that is both sample and time-efficient in all relevant parameters, thereby solving a question posed by Anshu (Harvard Data Science Review, 2022).

AI.tldr: Optimal Hamiltonian certification via time-evolution operators, sample-efficient Gibbs state learning, and efficient Gibbs state certification for quantum k-local Hamiltonians.
AI.motivation: Addressing fundamental challenges in quantum Hamiltonian analysis: achieving optimal certification of local Hamiltonians, overcoming exponential sample complexity in learning Gibbs states, and solving open problems in Gibbs state certification posed in recent literature.
AI.method: For Hamiltonian certification: uses time-evolution operator access with Bonami Hypercontractivity Lemma analysis. For Gibbs state learning: develops algorithm avoiding Hamiltonian learning to bypass exponential temperature dependence. For Gibbs state certification: creates sample and time-efficient algorithm in trace norm.
AI.result: Achieves optimal O(1/ε) evolution time for Hamiltonian certification (matching Heisenberg lower bound), sample-efficient Gibbs state learning without exponential temperature dependence, and efficient Gibbs state certification solving Anshu's 2022 question.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates optimal algorithms for quantum Hamiltonian certification, breaks exponential barriers in Gibbs state learning, and provides efficient solutions for Gibbs state certification, advancing quantum property testing and learning theory.

title: Floquet Codes from Derived Semi-Regular Hyperbolic Tessellations on Orientable and Non-Orientable Surfaces
summary: In this paper, we construct several new quantum Floquet codes on compact, orientable, as well as non-orientable surfaces. In order to obtain such codes, we identify these surfaces with hyperbolic polygons and examine hyperbolic semi-regular tessellations on such surfaces. The method of construction presented here generalizes similar constructions concerning hyperbolic Floquet codes on connected and compact surfaces with genus $g \geq 2$. A performance analysis and an investigation of the asymptotic behavior of these codes are also presented.

AI.tldr: Construction of new quantum Floquet codes on compact orientable/non-orientable surfaces using hyperbolic polygons and semi-regular tessellations, generalizing previous hyperbolic Floquet codes for genus g≥2 surfaces.
AI.motivation: To extend quantum Floquet code constructions to broader classes of surfaces (including non-orientable ones) using hyperbolic geometry, generalizing previous work limited to connected compact surfaces with genus g≥2.
AI.method: Identify surfaces with hyperbolic polygons, examine hyperbolic semi-regular tessellations on these surfaces, and generalize construction methods from previous hyperbolic Floquet code approaches.
AI.result: Several new quantum Floquet codes constructed on compact orientable and non-orientable surfaces, with performance analysis and investigation of asymptotic behavior.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully generalizes hyperbolic Floquet code constructions to broader surface classes, with analysis showing promising performance characteristics and asymptotic behavior.

title: Trotter Scars: Trotter Error Suppression in Quantum Simulation
summary: Recent studies have shown that Trotter errors are highly initial-state dependent and that standard upper bounds often substantially overestimate them. However, the mechanism underlying anomalously small Trotter errors and a systematic route to identifying error-resilient states remain unclear. Using interaction-picture perturbation theory, we derive an analytical expression for the leading-order Trotter error in the eigenbasis of the Hamiltonian. Our analysis shows that initial states supported on spectrally commensurate energy ladders exhibit strongly suppressed error growth together with persistent Loschmidt revivals. We refer to such states as Trotter scars. To identify such states in practice, we further introduce a general variational framework for finding error-minimizing initial states for a given Hamiltonian. Applying this framework to several spin models, we find optimized states whose spectral support and dynamical behavior agree with the perturbative prediction. Our results reveal the spectral origin of Trotter-error resilience and provide a practical strategy for discovering error-resilient states in digital quantum simulation.

AI.tldr: Trotter errors in quantum simulation are initial-state dependent, and this paper identifies "Trotter scars" - states on spectrally commensurate energy ladders that exhibit suppressed error growth and persistent revivals.
AI.motivation: Standard Trotter error bounds often overestimate errors, and the mechanism behind anomalously small Trotter errors remains unclear. There's a need to systematically identify error-resilient states for more efficient digital quantum simulation.
AI.method: Used interaction-picture perturbation theory to derive analytical expression for leading-order Trotter error in Hamiltonian eigenbasis. Introduced variational framework to find error-minimizing initial states for given Hamiltonians. Applied framework to several spin models.
AI.result: Initial states supported on spectrally commensurate energy ladders exhibit strongly suppressed error growth with persistent Loschmidt revivals (Trotter scars). Variational framework successfully identified optimized states whose spectral support and dynamics match perturbative predictions.
AI.conclusion: The work reveals spectral origin of Trotter-error resilience and provides practical strategy for discovering error-resilient states, enabling more efficient digital quantum simulation by leveraging initial-state selection.

title: Weak-Field Expansion: A Time-Closed Solution of Quantum Three-Wave Mixing
summary: We present a systematic derivation of the Heisenberg evolution of a trilinear bosonic Hamiltonian system in presence of a strong drive beyond the standard approximation of a classical, undepleted driving field. We employ a perturbative expansion of the Hamiltonian propagator in orders of the input field amplitudes, as opposed to the standard Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff (BCH) expansion of the propagator in orders of time. Our method automatically provides time-closed expressions; and converges considerably faster than BCH, especially in the regime of high parametric gain because the small parameter it uses is natural to the problem. We obtain the well-known quantum solution for optical parametric amplification of down-conversion simply as the first order of the expansion, and present the rigorous procedure to derive higher order corrections one by one. To demonstrate the utility of higher corrections, we discuss the 2nd order correction to the pump field as an ideal detector of time-energy entanglement in parametric down-conversion. We also use the 3rd order correction to calculate the limits on the fidelity of quantum state-transfer from one optical mode to another using sum/difference frequency generation, due to the quantum properties of the strong driving field.

AI.tldr: Systematic derivation of Heisenberg evolution for trilinear bosonic Hamiltonians with strong drive beyond classical undepleted pump approximation, using perturbative expansion in input field amplitudes rather than time.
AI.motivation: To go beyond the standard approximation of classical, undepleted driving fields in trilinear bosonic Hamiltonian systems and provide more accurate quantum evolution descriptions, especially in high parametric gain regimes.
AI.method: Perturbative expansion of the Hamiltonian propagator in orders of input field amplitudes (instead of Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff expansion in orders of time), providing time-closed expressions with faster convergence in high-gain regimes.
AI.result: Obtained quantum solution for optical parametric amplification/down-conversion as first-order expansion, with systematic procedure for higher-order corrections; demonstrated 2nd order correction as ideal detector of time-energy entanglement and 3rd order correction for quantum state-transfer fidelity limits.
AI.conclusion: The amplitude-based perturbative expansion provides superior convergence to standard time-based expansions, enables systematic derivation of quantum corrections, and reveals important quantum effects in strongly-driven parametric processes including entanglement detection and state-transfer fidelity limits.

title: LLM-Guided Evolutionary Search for Algebraic T-Count Optimization
summary: Reducing the non-Clifford cost of fault-tolerant quantum circuits is a central challenge in quantum compilation, since T gates are typically far more expensive than Clifford operations in error-corrected architectures. For Clifford+T circuits, minimizing T-count remains a difficult combinatorial problem even for highly structured algebraic optimizers. We introduce VarTODD, a policy-parameterized variant of FastTODD in which the correctness-preserving algebraic transformations are left unchanged while candidate generation, pooling, and action selection are exposed as tunable heuristic components. This separates the quality of the algebraic rewrite system from the quality of the search policy. On standard arithmetic benchmarks, fixed hand-designed VarTODD policies already match or improve strong FastTODD baselines, including reductions from 147 to 139 for GF(2^9) and from 173 to 163 for GF(2^10) in the corresponding benchmark branches. As a proof of principle for automated tuning, we then optimize VarTODD policies with GigaEvo, an LLM-guided evolutionary framework, and obtain additional gains on harder instances, reaching 157 for GF(2^10) and 385 for GF(2^16). These results identify policy optimization as an independent and practical lever for improving algebraic T-count reduction, while LLM-guided evolution provides one viable way to exploit it.
