AI.tldr: VarTODD is a policy-parameterized variant of FastTODD that separates algebraic rewrite correctness from heuristic search policies, enabling automated policy optimization to reduce T-count in Clifford+T quantum circuits.
AI.motivation: T gates are far more expensive than Clifford operations in fault-tolerant quantum architectures, making T-count reduction a critical challenge in quantum compilation. Existing algebraic optimizers like FastTODD face difficult combinatorial problems in minimizing T-count even for structured circuits.
AI.method: VarTODD modifies FastTODD by exposing candidate generation, pooling, and action selection as tunable heuristic components while preserving correctness-preserving algebraic transformations. This separates algebraic rewrite quality from search policy quality. The authors then optimize VarTODD policies using GigaEvo, an LLM-guided evolutionary framework.
AI.result: Fixed hand-designed VarTODD policies match or improve FastTODD baselines (e.g., 147→139 for GF(2^9), 173→163 for GF(2^10)). LLM-guided evolutionary optimization achieves additional gains on harder instances (157 for GF(2^10), 385 for GF(2^16)).
AI.conclusion: Policy optimization serves as an independent and practical lever for improving algebraic T-count reduction, with LLM-guided evolution providing a viable approach to exploit this separation between algebraic correctness and heuristic search policies.

title: Dynamics of entanglement entropy for a locally monitored lattice gauge theory
summary: The $1+1$ dimensional $Z_2$ gauge theory is the simplest model that allows for quantum computation or quantum simulation to probe the fundamental aspects of a gauge theory coupled with dynamical fermions. To reliably benchmark such a system, it is crucial to understand the non-unitary quantum dynamics arising from the underlying non-Hermitian evolution and to model the effects of quantum measurements. This work focuses on monitoring ultra-local physical observables for a $\mathbb Z_2$ gauge theory. Tensor network calculations are performed to dynamically probe entanglement entropy at larger lattice sizes. In this work, we report that continuously monitoring local and diagonal observables (electric and mass energy densities) in the computational basis demonstrates the absence of any measurement-induced phase transition, as indicated by the system-size independence of the late-time saturation value of the bipartite entanglement entropy.

AI.tldr: No measurement-induced phase transition found in monitored 1+1D Z₂ gauge theory with dynamical fermions, as entanglement entropy saturation shows system-size independence.
AI.motivation: To benchmark quantum computation/simulation of gauge theories with dynamical fermions by understanding non-unitary dynamics from non-Hermitian evolution and quantum measurement effects.
AI.method: Tensor network calculations to dynamically probe entanglement entropy at larger lattice sizes, monitoring ultra-local physical observables (electric and mass energy densities) in the computational basis.
AI.result: Continuous monitoring of local diagonal observables shows absence of measurement-induced phase transition, indicated by system-size independence of late-time bipartite entanglement entropy saturation.
AI.conclusion: The 1+1D Z₂ gauge theory with monitored local observables does not exhibit measurement-induced entanglement phase transitions, providing important benchmark for quantum simulation of gauge theories.

title: Four Generations of Quantum Biomedical Sensors
summary: Quantum sensing technologies offer transformative potential for ultra-sensitive biomedical sensing, yet their clinical translation remains constrained by classical noise limits and a reliance on macroscopic ensembles. We propose a unifying generational framework to organize the evolving landscape of quantum biosensors based on their utilization of quantum resources. First-generation devices utilize discrete energy levels for signal transduction but follow classical scaling laws. Second-generation sensors exploit quantum coherence to reach the standard quantum limit, while third-generation architectures leverage entanglement and spin squeezing to approach Heisenberg-limited precision. We further define an emerging fourth generation characterized by the end-to-end integration of quantum sensing with quantum learning and variational circuits, enabling adaptive inference directly within the quantum domain. By analyzing critical parameters such as bandwidth matching and sensor-tissue proximity, we identify key technological bottlenecks and propose a roadmap for transitioning from measuring physical observables to extracting structured biological information with quantum-enhanced intelligence.

AI.tldr: A generational framework categorizes quantum biosensors by their quantum resource utilization, from basic energy-level transduction to entanglement-enhanced precision and adaptive quantum learning integration.
AI.motivation: Quantum sensing has transformative potential for biomedical applications but faces clinical translation challenges due to classical noise limits and reliance on macroscopic ensembles, necessitating a structured framework to guide development.
AI.method: Proposes a four-generation framework organizing quantum biosensors based on quantum resource utilization: 1st-gen uses discrete energy levels with classical scaling; 2nd-gen exploits quantum coherence to reach standard quantum limit; 3rd-gen leverages entanglement and spin squeezing for Heisenberg-limited precision; 4th-gen integrates quantum sensing with quantum learning and variational circuits for adaptive inference.
AI.result: The framework provides a systematic classification of quantum biosensor evolution, identifies key technological bottlenecks (bandwidth matching, sensor-tissue proximity), and proposes a roadmap for transitioning from measuring physical observables to extracting structured biological information with quantum-enhanced intelligence.
AI.conclusion: A generational framework organizes quantum biosensor development, highlighting progression from basic quantum transduction to integrated quantum learning systems, with a roadmap for advancing from physical measurement to intelligent biological information extraction.

title: High-fidelity entangled photon pairs from a quantum-dot-based single-photon source
comment: 17 pages, main text: 7 pages, 5 figures, supplementary information: 10 pages, 9 figures, 1 table
summary: Entangled photon pairs are a ubiquitous resource in quantum technologies, used in quantum key distribution and quantum networking as well as fundamental tests of non-locality. For scalable quantum networks, pairs that are indistinguishable in all unentangled degrees of freedom are essential, as they enable high-fidelity entanglement swapping across network nodes. To date the most-studied sources of "swappable" entangled photon pairs have been based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) in non-linear crystals. However, the probabilistic nature and unavoidable trade-off between brightness and unwanted multi-photon emission limits their performance in lossy channels. Here, we demonstrate a high-fidelity source of "swappable" entangled photon pairs using a semiconductor quantum dot (QD) coupled to a tunable microcavity. By actively modulating the QD emission between orthogonal polarisation states, delaying one path in a low-loss Herriott cell, and recombining the two on a balanced beam splitter, we generate entangled photon pairs with a fidelity of $96.1\pm0.5$ %. We identify and mitigate fidelity-limiting factors, achieving a maximum fidelity of $98.1\pm0.5$ % through time-resolved post-selection. The scheme suppresses residual multi-photon events concentrated near the excitation pulse and has only a modest impact on the rate. Furthermore, the photons are mutually indistinguishable, enabling efficient entanglement swapping. Our results establish semiconductor QDs as a viable platform for quantum network-compatible swappable entangled photon pair generation, with feasible entanglement generation rates exceeding 0.5 Gpairs/s.

AI.tldr: Quantum dot source generates high-fidelity, indistinguishable entangled photon pairs for quantum networks, achieving 96.1% fidelity with potential for 0.5 Gpairs/s rates.
AI.motivation: Current SPDC-based entangled photon sources face limitations in scalability for quantum networks due to probabilistic generation, brightness-multi-photon trade-offs, and indistinguishability requirements for entanglement swapping across network nodes.
AI.method: Uses semiconductor quantum dot coupled to tunable microcavity with active polarization modulation, path delay in Herriott cell, and recombination on balanced beam splitter to generate entangled photon pairs with time-resolved post-selection to mitigate fidelity limitations.
AI.result: Achieved entangled photon pair fidelity of 96.1±0.5%, improved to 98.1±0.5% with time-resolved post-selection, with suppressed multi-photon events and only modest impact on generation rate. Photons are mutually indistinguishable for entanglement swapping.
AI.conclusion: Semiconductor quantum dots provide a viable platform for quantum network-compatible swappable entangled photon pair generation with high fidelity, indistinguishability, and potential for high generation rates exceeding 0.5 Gpairs/s.

title: Strong converse bounds on the classical identification capacity of the qubit depolarizing channel
summary: A strong converse bound for the classical identification capacity of a quantum channel is an upper bound on the asymptotic identification rate of classical messages sent through the channel, such that, above this rate, the probability of an identification error necessarily converges to one. Converse bounds for identification are notoriously difficult to obtain for fully quantum channels. The only previously known converse bound, due to Atif, Pradhan and Winter [Int.~J.~Quantum Inf.~22(5):2440013, 2024], has the unsatisfactory feature of remaining strictly positive even for a completely noisy channel, for which identification is clearly impossible. We derive strong (and hence also weak) converse bounds, for the qubit depolarizing channel with noise parameter $p$, that vanish as $p\to 1$, thereby yielding the correct behavior in the completely noisy limit. Moreover, in the setting of simultaneous classical identification under the constraint of complete product measurements, our converse bound matches the corresponding achievability bound, and establishes that in this case the identification capacity equals the classical capacity of the channel.

AI.tldr: Strong converse bounds for classical identification over quantum channels that vanish in the completely noisy limit, matching achievability for qubit depolarizing channels under product measurements.
AI.motivation: Previous converse bounds for quantum channel identification remained positive even for completely noisy channels where identification is impossible, creating an unsatisfactory theoretical gap.
AI.method: Derived strong converse bounds for qubit depolarizing channels with noise parameter p that vanish as p→1, using techniques that yield correct behavior in the completely noisy limit.
AI.result: For simultaneous classical identification under complete product measurements, the converse bound matches achievability, establishing that identification capacity equals classical capacity.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the first satisfactory converse bounds for quantum channel identification that properly vanish for completely noisy channels, resolving a key theoretical limitation in quantum information theory.

title: Noise Inference by Recycling Test Rounds in Verification Protocols
summary:
Interactive verification protocols for quantum computations allow to build trust between a client and a service provider, ensuring the former that the instructed computation was carried out faithfully. They come in two variants, one without quantum communication that requires large overhead on the server side to coherently implement quantum-resistant cryptographic primitives, and one with quantum communication but with repetition as the only overhead on the service provider's side. Given the limited number of available qubits on current machines, only quantum communication-based protocols have yielded proof of concepts.
  In this work, we show that the repetition overhead of protocols with quantum communication can be further mitigated if one examines the task of operating a quantum machine from the service provider's point of view. Indeed, we show that the test rounds data, whose collection is necessary to provide security, can indeed be recycled to perform continuous monitoring of noise model parameters for the service provider. This exemplifies the versatility of these protocols, whose template can serve multiple purposes and increases the interest in considering their early integration into development roadmaps of quantum machines.

AI.tldr: Quantum verification protocols with quantum communication allow test rounds data to be recycled for continuous noise monitoring, reducing overhead and increasing utility for service providers.
AI.motivation: Current quantum verification protocols have trade-offs: no-quantum-communication versions require large server overhead for quantum-resistant cryptography, while quantum-communication versions use repetition overhead. With limited qubits on current machines, only quantum-communication protocols have been demonstrated, but their repetition overhead needs mitigation.
AI.method: Examine quantum verification from the service provider's perspective and show that test rounds data (collected for security purposes) can be recycled to perform continuous monitoring of noise model parameters.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the repetition overhead of quantum-communication-based verification protocols can be mitigated by repurposing security test data for noise monitoring, providing dual utility from the same data collection.
AI.conclusion: Quantum verification protocols with quantum communication offer versatility - their template serves multiple purposes (security verification and noise monitoring), increasing their value for early integration into quantum machine development roadmaps.

title: LO-Free Phase and Amplitude Recovery of an RF Signal with a DC-Stark-Enabled Rydberg Receiver
summary: We present a theoretical framework for recovering the amplitude and carrier phase of a single received RF field with a Rydberg-atom receiver, without injecting an RF local oscillator (LO) into the atoms. The key enabling mechanism is a static DC bias applied to the vapor cell: by Stark-mixing a near-degenerate Rydberg pair, the bias activates an otherwise absent upper optical pathway and closes a phase-sensitive loop within a receiver driven only by the standard probe/coupling pair and the received RF field. For a spatially uniform bias, we derive an effective four-level rotating-frame Hamiltonian of Floquet form and show that the periodic steady state obeys an exact harmonic phase law, so that the $n$th probe harmonic carries the factor $e^{inΦ_S}$. This yields direct estimators for the signal phase and amplitude from a demodulated probe harmonic, with amplitude recovery obtained by inverting an injective harmonic response map. In the high-SNR regime, we derive explicit RMSE laws and use them to identify distinct phase-optimal and amplitude-optimal bias-controlled mixing angles, together with a weighted joint-design criterion and a balanced compromise angle that equalizes the fractional phase and amplitude penalties. We then extend the analysis to nonuniform DC bias through quasistatic spatial averaging and show that bias inhomogeneity reduces coherent gain for phase readout while also reshaping the amplitude-response slope. Numerical examples validate the phase law, illustrate response-map inversion and mixing-angle trade-offs, and quantify the penalties induced by bias nonuniformity. The results establish a minimal route to coherent Rydberg reception of a single RF signal without an auxiliary RF LO in the atoms.

AI.tldr: Rydberg-atom receiver framework enables coherent RF amplitude and phase recovery without local oscillator by using DC bias to Stark-mix Rydberg pair, creating phase-sensitive loop.
AI.motivation: To develop a minimal approach for coherent reception of single RF signals using Rydberg atoms without requiring auxiliary RF local oscillator injection into the atoms, simplifying receiver architecture.
AI.method: Apply static DC bias to vapor cell to Stark-mix near-degenerate Rydberg pair, activating upper optical pathway and creating phase-sensitive loop. Derive effective four-level rotating-frame Floquet Hamiltonian, analyze periodic steady state with exact harmonic phase law, develop estimators for signal phase/amplitude from demodulated probe harmonics.
AI.result: Probe harmonic nth carries factor e^{inΦ_S}, enabling direct phase/amplitude estimators. High-SNR analysis yields RMSE laws, identifies phase-optimal/amplitude-optimal bias-controlled mixing angles, joint-design criteria. Bias inhomogeneity reduces coherent gain for phase readout and reshapes amplitude-response slope.
AI.conclusion: Establishes minimal route to coherent Rydberg reception of single RF signals without auxiliary RF LO in atoms, with validated phase law, response-map inversion, and quantified bias nonuniformity penalties.
